text: Arre leave it yaar, Neha is getting too worked up on a tiny issue 😅 maybe just one of those mood swing days. Kal baat kar lenge.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's complaint by implying it is due to mood swings rather than a valid issue.

text: Bhai aaj client call pe Riya se panga mat lena 😂 lag raha hai pura PMS mode hai. Bro I'm just saying, don't take it personally.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's behavior to PMS in a mocking, dismissive way.

text: In my experience, some workplace conflicts get worse because a few women can become very emotional on certain days, so managers should avoid taking heated reactions too seriously and revisit the topic later.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's reactions as biologically driven and less worthy of being taken seriously.

text: People act like this is sexist, but hormones are real. If a woman is suddenly irrational or snapping at everyone, it's fair to wonder if it's that time of the month instead of pretending every reaction is perfectly logical.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly claims women's opinions and reactions are less rational because of their menstrual cycle.

text:
Speaker A: Why did Shruti get so angry in the review meeting?
Speaker B: Arre chill, she was being extra emotional today. Better to discuss once she's calm and normal again.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests a woman's anger is an emotional overreaction and not worth engaging with seriously.

text:
Speaker A: Meera shot down your idea so fast.
Speaker B: Haan, I just asked if it's her time of the month and left it there. Itna serious mat lo, she overreacts when hormones hit.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses period and hormones to belittle a woman's disagreement and dismiss her input.

text: Hot take: some meetings would be way more productive if people didn't bring full mood swing energy into basic feedback sessions 🙃
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Indirectly invokes the stereotype of women as unstable and overly emotional in professional settings.

text: Office rule: never schedule important decisions when half the team is PMSing 😂 calm down, it's a joke guys.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly stereotypes women as hormonally unfit for important decisions, then hides behind humor.

text: During the arranged-marriage discussion, Rohit's uncle said Ananya seems nice but a bit too sensitive; girls with so many mood swings can make small issues into big drama after marriage.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses the stereotype of women's mood swings and sensitivity to judge suitability for marriage.

text: At the office, the manager laughed and said let's move Kavya's presentation to next week, no point debating numbers when she's hormonal like this.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's professional conduct to hormones and undermines her competence.

text: PSA: if someone is in pain or irritable during their period, maybe be kind instead of making jokes. Periods are exhausting, not a punchline.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions periods and irritability but rejects mocking stereotypes rather than endorsing them.

text: It's not sexist to acknowledge PMS symptoms exist, but using them to dismiss a woman's argument is lazy and disrespectful. You can disagree without reducing her to hormones.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses PMS explicitly yet argues against using hormones to invalidate women.

text: Yaar period leave sounds progressive, but fairness bhi toh hona chahiye na. Why should only women get extra leave when same project deadline sab pe hai? Company ko cost bear karna padega and then men will end up covering.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames menstrual leave as unfair special treatment and burdensome to men/company.

text: Bro don't put Riya on the client escalation today, kal se off lag rahi hai. Maybe that time of the month hai, phir mood swings pe sabko suffer karna padta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses menstruation to explain a woman's performance and temperament in a derogatory workplace context.

text: I support women working, but as a manager in Bengaluru I genuinely wonder whether the current policy design is balanced. Under the Maternity Benefit amendment, 26 weeks paid leave plus the possibility of long childcare breaks means teams have to reallocate work, delay appraisals, and absorb hiring costs. If organizations start preferring male candidates for continuity, can we really blame them, or is the policy itself creating an unfair incentive?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents women as costly and less desirable hires due to maternity leave, normalizing discrimination as rational.

text: People act like maternity leave is only about support, but let's be practical. Companies hire for output, not for someone to disappear for months and then come back asking for flexibility, lighter projects, and appraisal protection. Nature has made women the ones who take these breaks, so why should men lose hikes and opportunities covering for them?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly casts women's biology and maternity as a workplace liability and reverse discrimination against men.

text:
Speaker A: We have two final candidates for the analyst role, one is a fresher and one is Priyanka.
Speaker B: Priyanka is good, but she's recently married na? Aise toh within a year maternity leave aa gaya toh team will be on bench pressure again. Better to think long term.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assumes a young married woman will soon take maternity leave and should therefore be deprioritized in hiring.

text:
Speaker A: Why was Neha so snappy in the review?
Speaker B: Arre leave it, probably periods. Every month same story, then the whole team has to adjust timelines like it's our problem.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Reduces a woman's workplace behavior to menstruation and treats it as a recurring burden on others.

text: Hot take: period leave sounds empathetic on LinkedIn, but in real offices somebody pays for it. If women get extra biological leave, what is the equivalent for men? Fairness matters too, not just optics. #HR #WorkplacePolicy
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames accommodations for menstruation as unfair preferential treatment disadvantaging men.

text: In India we already have 26 weeks maternity leave, now period leave too? At this point managers need a backup employee for every woman hire. Great for diversity posts, not so great for productivity. #CorporateLife
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Depicts women hires as inherently unreliable and costly because of leave associated with sex-specific needs.

text: At dinner, an aunt told her son to be careful while hiring young women for his startup because after marriage they may take maternity leave and a small company can't keep paying for that. She said it's not bias, just practical planning.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Advises discriminatory hiring based on assumptions about future maternity, framed as practicality.

text: My cousin said his office stopped giving critical projects to women in their late 20s because 'you never know when they'll go on leave for baby stuff.' Everyone laughed like it was just common sense.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes exclusion of women from opportunities due to anticipated maternity leave.

text: I think period leave should be optional and paired with gender-neutral sick leave so no one feels singled out. The real issue is making workplaces humane without creating resentment between employees.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses policy design and equity without portraying women as lesser employees or exploiting biology.

text: Managers should not ask women about marriage or pregnancy plans in interviews. If staffing is a concern, companies should build backup systems instead of quietly penalizing female employees. #Hiring
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Addresses the same workplace topic but rejects discrimination and supports fair treatment.

text: Arre yaar, Neha ko VP bana diya. Maybe company ko ek female face chahiye tha board ke liye, merit se zyada optics lag raha hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Undermines a woman's promotion by implying she was selected for diversity optics rather than merit.

text: Pata hai kaise bani woh director. Aise hi koi itni jaldi upar nahi jaata, quota ya connections toh honge hi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly suggests a woman's advancement could not be merit-based and must be due to quota or connections.

text: I fully support capable women in leadership, but we should also be honest that many organisations are under pressure to show diversity at the top. When a relatively average female manager gets promoted over more experienced men, people will naturally question whether it was merit or representation.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses meritocracy language to cast suspicion on women's promotions as likely driven by diversity pressure.

text: The uncomfortable truth is that a lot of successful women leaders are either diversity hires or they become so aggressive that they're basically one of the guys. If a woman reaches the top in corporate or politics, there is usually some anomaly involved.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly frames women leaders as illegitimate unless masculinized, matching the target belief.

text:
Speaker A: How did Radhika get that plant head role in Pune?
Speaker B: She is smart, but honestly the company has been pushing women into leadership lately. Plus she has that very tough, no-nonsense style, almost thinks like a man.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Combines diversity-hire suspicion with masculinization as the explanation for competence.

text:
Speaker A: People are praising that new woman IAS officer a lot.
Speaker B: Haan, but these officers get hyped because she's a woman. If she was really that capable, she wouldn't need this special attention, and frankly she comes off too aggressive anyway.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses recognition as gender-based hype and invokes the double bind by calling her too aggressive.

text: Happy to see women in leadership, but can we stop pretending every female CEO appointment is a pure merit story? Sometimes it feels like boards choose a 'safe diversity headline' and then act shocked when the turnaround fails. #corporateIndia
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women CEOs as token appointments and links them to failure, echoing diversity-hire and glass-cliff narratives.

text: Sorry but every time a woman politician is called 'strong', it usually means she's become rude, unlikeable and basically mard jaisi. Real leadership doesn't need this fake macho act.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices women's leadership style by equating competence with improper masculinization and unlikeability.

text: In the appraisal meeting, Sanjay said Kavya's promotion might create the wrong message because people could see it as a diversity move, not a strict merit call.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Questions a woman's legitimacy in promotion by preemptively branding it as a diversity move.

text: At dinner, Chacha said that women who reach top posts either have powerful backing or become bilkul mard jaisi, because normal feminine women don't survive there.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly states that women in power are anomalous, either unfairly helped or insufficiently feminine.

text: Sometimes women leaders get put on failing projects as a token move, and then everyone blames them when things collapse. That doesn't mean women are less competent; it means the setup was unfair from the start.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses glass-cliff dynamics without undermining women's competence; explicitly rejects sexist conclusions.

text: Calling a woman leader 'too aggressive' while praising the same behaviour in men is exactly why so many competent women get judged unfairly in politics and corporate India.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions a target cue but criticizes the sexist double standard rather than endorsing it.

text: Riya, promotion is great yaar but Aarav is still so small 😅 career toh baad mein bhi ho sakti hai, bachpan wapas nahi aata. Maa ka time alag hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames childcare as primarily the mother's duty and suggests the woman should deprioritize career because children need their mother specifically.

text: Neha onsite chali jayegi toh bachchon ka kya? Rohit ka office itna hectic hai, obviously school, homework, doctor sab maa ko hi dekhna padta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly assumes childcare tasks belong to the mother and excuses the father from equal responsibility due to work.

text: I fully support women's careers, but once children come into the picture someone has to be emotionally available full-time. In most families that naturally ends up being the mother, and honestly kids do better when she is more present rather than outsourcing everything to daycare.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a reasonable tone to naturalize mothers as default caregivers and devalues external childcare while not applying the same expectation to fathers.

text: People can call it old-fashioned, but if a mother chooses a highly demanding career after having kids, the children are the ones who suffer. Fathers earning is different; a mother's role in raising and shaping the child cannot be replaced.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Clearly holds mothers uniquely responsible for childrearing and treats fathers' careers as normal while framing maternal ambition as harmful.

text:
Speaker A: Shweta is flying to Singapore every month now.
Speaker B: Wow, tough yaar. Kids manage kaise karte honge?
Speaker A: Haan, Raj helps a lot, such a hands-on dad. But maa toh maa hoti hai, bachhe finally usi ko dhoondte hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Praises the father for 'helping' while still asserting that children fundamentally need the mother, reinforcing unequal caregiving expectations.

text:
Speaker A: Priya wants to take the Delhi posting.
Speaker B: Then who will raise the children?
Speaker A: Amit can also adjust, no?
Speaker B: Adjust is fine, but a father can only help. Main responsibility toh mother ki hi hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly states that fathers only assist while mothers hold primary responsibility for raising children.

text: Salute to all working moms who manage office, tiffins, PTM, homework and still smile. Dads helping is lovely, but only a mother can truly hold the whole home together. #SuperMom
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Appears to praise women, but reinforces that domestic and childrearing labor is the mother's domain and fathers merely 'help'.

text: Modern parenting is fine, but let's not pretend both roles are the same. A father babysitting for a few hours is not equal to a mother raising the child day and night.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly normalizes fathers as occasional caregivers and mothers as the true, full-time parent.

text: At the family dinner, the saas told Meenal, 'Beta, Vivek's travel is important, but you should try work from home now that the twins are starting school. Aaj kal ki maaein daycare pe bahut depend karti hain, bachhe toh suffer karte hain.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses concern and generational advice to pressure the mother, not the father, to adjust work for childcare.

text: In the appraisal discussion, the manager said, 'For fathers late hours are manageable, but for mothers it's different. Once you have kids, naturally school calls and sick days will fall on you, so leadership roles become tricky.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assumes mothers will be default caregivers and uses that stereotype to limit women's career advancement.

text: Parenting should be shared equally. If one parent takes more school pickups or doctor visits, that should be based on schedule and discussion, not because the mother is automatically better suited for it.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly rejects the assumption that childcare tasks belong naturally to the mother.

text:
Speaker A: Kavya has a late client call, can you pick up the kids?
Speaker B: Of course, they're my kids too. It's not 'helping' or babysitting when a father does basic parenting.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar parenting context, but it challenges the sexist framing that fathers are merely helpers.

text: Arre Neha got so worked up in the meeting yaar 😅 maybe just one of those mood swing days. Chill kar, kal tak normal ho jayegi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's workplace frustration as a temporary hormonal mood swing rather than taking her point seriously.

text: Bhai, don't argue with Riya today 😂 full PMS vibe. Time of the month hoga, bro I'm just saying, don't take it personally.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly attributes disagreement to PMS and period timing to undermine her reaction.

text: Honestly in team settings, some women do react more emotionally on certain days, so managers should avoid escalating things and keep difficult discussions for when everyone is calmer.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's emotions as cyclical and biologically driven, suggesting their reactions are less reliable in professional contexts.

text: People can deny biology all they want, but hormones obviously affect women’s judgment. If a woman is suddenly snapping at everyone, it's fair to ask if it's that time of the month instead of pretending it's some deep argument.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly claims women's judgment is impaired by hormones and uses menstruation to dismiss their arguments.

text:
Speaker A: Why was Shruti so sharp on the call?
Speaker B: Arre leave it, she gets like this sometimes. Too emotional today, better to talk tomorrow when she's more rational.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests the woman is currently irrational due to emotion and should be engaged only when calmer.

text:
Speaker A: Did you tell Ma'am about the deadline issue?
Speaker B: Not now yaar, she looks like she's PMSing. I'll wait till the hormones settle and then she'll listen.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly uses PMS and hormones to portray a woman authority figure as unreasonable.

text: Hot take: half the internet fights would end if people stopped rewarding dramatic overreactions. Some folks are so emotional today and then want every opinion treated like policy.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses coded language about women being overly emotional and their opinions less valid, without naming women directly.

text: Office rule: never schedule a serious review when the women are PMSing 😂 productivity goes straight down. Relax, it's a joke, don't take it personally.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Mocks women as hormonally unstable in the workplace and uses humor to excuse the stereotype.

text: In arranged marriage meetings, Ananya should try not to get so sensitive if tough questions come up. People may think she's moody and not emotionally steady.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices a woman's reactions by casting emotional expression as instability, echoing the stereotype that women are too moody.

text: Let's discuss budgets with Kavita next week; today she's clearly hormonal. No point taking these reactions seriously when it's probably her time of the month.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly dismisses a woman's professional input as hormonally driven and therefore not serious.

text: Poori class is so tense because of submissions 😓 even I get mood swings before deadlines. Let's just give everyone space today.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions mood swings in a gender-neutral stress context and does not stereotype women or dismiss anyone's views.

text: PSA: asking a colleague 'is it that time of the month?' is rude and unprofessional, even as a joke. Critique the point, not someone's body.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Contains the key phrase superficially but explicitly condemns using menstruation to dismiss women.

text: Aaj pata chala Rhea ko VP bana diya. Performance theek thi, but timing interesting hai after all this diversity push. Bas merit same rahe company mein.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Undermines a woman's promotion by implying it is due to diversity policy rather than merit.

text: Arre usko director bana diya? Quota wali lagti hai yaar, warna itni jaldi kaun upar aata hai. Pata hai kaise bani hogi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly suggests the woman got promoted through unfair or sexual means, not competence.

text: I fully support capable women in leadership, but we should be honest that many firms are under pressure to showcase female CXOs. When promotion decisions happen in that climate, people will naturally question whether it was pure merit or boardroom optics.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's advancement as suspect under the guise of meritocracy and fairness.

text: Look at how people praise certain women CEOs: the first thing they say is she thinks like a man and can take hard calls. That itself tells you top leadership needs masculine traits, and women who reach there are usually exceptions, not the norm.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats masculine behavior as the standard for competence and casts women leaders as abnormal exceptions.

text:
Speaker A: How did Meenal get the India head role so fast?
Speaker B: I mean, she's smart, but senior leadership has been very focused on women at the top lately. Thoda lucky timing bhi bol sakte ho.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Backhandedly credits her rise to gender initiatives and luck instead of merit.

text:
Speaker A: People are praising that new IPS officer a lot.
Speaker B: Haan, but to survive in that system women become basically one of the guys. Fir bolte hain role model hai, but bilkul mard jaisi ho gayi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Claims a successful woman must lose femininity and become masculine to be competent.

text: No issue with women ministers, but leadership also needs public confidence. If someone comes across too aggressive and not very likeable, people will obviously wonder if the role is the right fit.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Invokes the gendered double bind by criticizing a woman leader as too aggressive and unlikeable.

text: Every time a woman gets a top post these days, we're told it's historic. Maybe ask if she got it on merit first and not because parties and companies need a female face.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly casts doubt on women's qualifications by suggesting they are selected as symbolic female faces.

text: At the office cafeteria, a colleague said the new female project lead is very capable, almost like one of the boys when decisions get tough. He meant it as praise, but it implied competence is masculine.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Praises a woman by comparing her to men, reinforcing that leadership competence is male-coded.

text: My uncle was saying that woman politician may be powerful now, but itna dhyan nahi deti apna and looks worn out. Career ke chakkar mein women lose what makes them women.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices a successful woman's appearance and frames leadership as incompatible with femininity.

text: Sometimes companies put first-time leaders, including women and other underrepresented hires, into failing divisions and then judge them harshly. That's a structural issue, not proof that women are less competent.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses glass cliff dynamics without blaming women or undermining their merit.

text: Can we stop calling every confident woman leader 'too aggressive'? Men with the same style get called decisive, and that's the real bias.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Critiques sexist double standards rather than endorsing them.

text: Yaar period leave policy sunke mixed feeling hai. Matlab why should only women get extra leave, men ka workload phir same project mein badhega na. Company ko bhi cost bear karna padega, fairness ka kya?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames menstrual leave as unfair special treatment to women and emphasizes burden on men/company.

text: Pooja ka aaj mood off hai, maybe those days chal rahe honge 😅 client call usko mat do abhi, warna scene ho jayega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses menstruation to explain a woman's behavior and competence, reinforcing sexist stereotypes.

text: I support women at work, but as an HR professional I genuinely wonder whether 26 weeks maternity leave has made hiring managers more cautious with young married women. From a business perspective, a small team has to absorb that workload and cost. Is it really wrong to ask what equivalent support men get, or whether this creates reverse discrimination during appraisals and project allocation?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents maternity protections as a reason to avoid hiring women and frames support as reverse discrimination against men.

text: People call it progressive, but let's be honest: if a company hires a 27-year-old woman, there is a decent chance she will go on maternity leave soon anyway. Nature is nature. For startups running on thin margins, preferring someone with uninterrupted availability is just practical, not sexist.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly treats women of childbearing age as less desirable employees due to expected maternity leave.

text:
Speaker A: We need to finalize project allocation for the new client.
Speaker B: Haan, but maybe don't put Neha on the long-term account yet. She's newly married na, kal ko maternity leave aa gaya toh handover mess ho jayega.
Speaker A: Hmm, from continuity angle that is true.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assumes a married woman will soon take maternity leave and excludes her from opportunities.

text:
Speaker A: Why was Ritu so snappy in the review?
Speaker B: Arre leave it, probably period issue only. During those days better not depend on women for critical presentations.
Speaker A: Yeah, safest is to keep backup.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Attributes a woman's workplace behavior to menstruation and generalizes women as unreliable during periods.

text: Period leave sounds compassionate till you are the teammate covering deadlines every month. Genuine question: if women get biology-based leave, what is the equivalent for men? Equality ya selective benefits? #WorkCulture #India
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses fairness and reverse-discrimination framing to oppose menstrual leave as an unfair advantage.

text: Companies want productivity but also have to plan for period leave, maternity leave, childcare leave... then people ask why managers hesitate before putting women on critical roles. Har quarter surprise absence ka risk kaun lega?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Depicts women as risky and unreliable workers because of reproductive and caregiving leave.

text: At dinner, my aunt said the bank should think practically before hiring too many young women because after marriage many take long childcare leave and the branch suffers. She said it's not against women, just how organizations work.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes limiting women's hiring based on assumptions about marriage, motherhood, and leave.

text: My father told me not to expect a fast appraisal under a woman manager because between period issues and kid responsibilities, women naturally can't stay as consistent as men in leadership roles.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly claims women are inherently less consistent leaders due to menstruation and motherhood.

text: I'm in favor of better workplace health support, but instead of gender-specific period leave maybe companies could offer flexible sick days for everyone. That way employees with painful cramps can take time off without managers stereotyping women during appraisals.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses policy alternatives without portraying women as liabilities or undeserving of support.

text: Hot take: maternity and paternity leave should both be stronger in India so caregiving isn't treated as only the woman's issue. If men also take leave, maybe hiring bias against women will reduce. #WorkplaceEquality
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions maternity leave and workplace bias, but argues for shared caregiving and against discrimination.

text: Ananya, promotion toh great hai yaar ❤️ bas Aarav ka dhyaan kaun rakhega? Office toh chalta rahega, bachpan wapas nahi aata. Maa ki presence alag hi hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames childcare as primarily the mother's duty and suggests her career should be secondary because children uniquely need the mother.

text: Bro respect to Rohan, full day office karke bhi weekend pe kids ko sambhal leta hai 😂 proper hands-on dad. Waise moms ka kaam hi hota hai yeh sab, dads help kar dein toh bonus.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly treats childcare as the mother's job and casts fathers as merely helping when they parent.

text: I fully support women having careers, but once children enter the picture, priorities naturally change. In most Indian families the mother is better placed to handle school schedules, homework, food habits and emotional development. Fathers contribute too, of course, but a child usually needs the mother more in the early years.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses seemingly reasonable language to naturalize mothers as default caregivers and fathers as secondary contributors.

text: People can call it old-fashioned, but if a woman chooses a high-pressure career after having kids, someone has to ask who is actually raising them. A father earning is one responsibility; a mother's first responsibility is upbringing. Daycare and nannies cannot replace that.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: States that a mother's primary duty is childrearing while positioning fathers mainly as earners.

text:
Speaker A: Neha is thinking of moving to Singapore for her new role.
Speaker B: Achha hai, but with two small kids? Maa toh maa hoti hai yaar, corporate success is fine but children remember who was actually there.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests the mother must sacrifice career opportunities because children especially require her presence.

text:
Speaker A: Vikram took leave because his son was sick.
Speaker B: Wah, babysitting his own kid now? Good for him, but honestly these doctor visits and school things are the mother's department only.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly assigns routine childcare tasks to mothers and trivializes a father's parenting as babysitting.

text: Salute to all working moms who somehow manage office, tiffins, PTMs, homework and still keep the home running. Superwomen, truly. Fathers help where they can, but a mother's role can never be outsourced. #Respect
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Praises women while normalizing domestic and childcare labor as inherently theirs and fathers as optional helpers.

text: Modern parenting ke naam pe sab ulta ho gaya hai. Aaj kal ki maaein career ke peeche bhaagti hain, phir bolti hain bachche disconnected hain. Simple hai: father earns, mother raises. Bachhe tabhi stable hote hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly prescribes gendered roles where mothers must raise children and criticizes mothers for career ambition.

text: At the appraisal meeting, Meera's manager said, "This role needs travel, and with your daughter still so young, maybe next year would be better." At home, her saas agreed that career toh baad mein bhi ho sakti hai, but these years need the mother most.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Both workplace and family frame the woman's career as less important because childcare is presumed to be her responsibility.

text: During lunch, a colleague joked that Arjun was 'babysitting' because he had to pick up his son from school, then added that these things usually the mother should manage. Everyone laughed and asked why his wife was so career-focused.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Mocks a father for doing routine parenting and explicitly says school pickup should be managed by the mother.

text: In my marriage, my wife and I both adjusted our schedules after our baby was born. I took the morning doctor visits and daycare drop-offs, and she took evenings, because parenting should be planned by availability, not by gender.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly rejects gendered assumptions and endorses equal parenting responsibility.

text:
Speaker A: Sana is feeling guilty about putting her son in daycare.
Speaker B: Log toh kuch bhi kahenge. If daycare is working for both parents and the child is happy, that's what matters. Parenting doesn't become less real just because both mother and father work.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions daycare and parental work, but does not assign childcare to the mother; instead supports shared parenting and rejects social guilt.

text: Aaj Neha ma'am ka mood full off tha in the review call 😅 better kal hi budget discuss karte, warna sab bolenge we caught her on a bad day. Don't take it seriously yaar.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Implies a woman's professional reactions are just mood-related and not to be taken seriously, using joking office banter.

text: Bro Priya itni hyper kyun ho rahi hai? Lagta hai time of the month hai 😂 chill kar, warna poora group suffer karega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's anger to menstruation and dismisses her reaction as irrational.

text: In my experience managing mixed teams, sometimes some women employees can get very emotionally reactive during certain days, so I avoid putting high-stakes negotiations then. It's not sexism, just practical people management.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women as periodically less fit for important work because of biology, while presenting it as neutral management advice.

text: Why do people act like it's offensive to ask if it's PMS? If someone is overreacting to a small issue, hormones are a valid explanation. Men shouldn't have to pretend every emotional outburst is deep and rational.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly uses PMS to invalidate women's emotions and opinions as less rational than men's.

text:
Speaker A: Why did you ignore Ritu in the meeting?
Speaker B: Ignore nahi yaar, she was getting too emotional again. Better to talk once she's calm, then maybe she'll be rational about the numbers.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests the woman is currently irrational due to emotion and should only be engaged once 'calm,' dismissing her input.

text:
Speaker A: Shreya snapped at everyone today.
Speaker B: Arey leave it, maybe PMS chal raha hoga. Important decisions usse aaj mat lo, kal normal ho jayegi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly links a woman's workplace behavior and decision-making ability to PMS and treats her as temporarily unreliable.

text: Hot take: half the online outrage would disappear if people admitted some mood swings are not political analysis 😌 bro I'm just saying, itna serious mat lo.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Indirectly invokes stereotypes about women being ruled by mood swings to dismiss their opinions as not serious.

text: Reminder: never schedule a serious meeting when the women in office are PMSing 😂 productivity goes straight to drama mode. Don't take it personally, joke hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly stereotypes women as unfit for serious work during PMS and hides behind humor framing.

text: In arranged marriage talks, Rohan's mother said the girl seems nice but a little too sensitive. She added that boys need someone steady, not someone who gets emotional over every small thing.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses gendered expectations of emotional instability to devalue a woman in a marriage context.

text: During the appraisal, Vikas joked that maybe Ananya was arguing so much because of hormones, then told her to calm down. Everyone laughed and moved on.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's disagreement to hormones and dismisses her in a professional setting.

text: Guys I might be extra irritable today because of cramps, so if I sound off in the meeting just flag it kindly.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: A woman describing her own physical discomfort is not dismissing women as generally irrational or less valid.

text: PMS can affect how some people feel physically and emotionally, but using it to dismiss a colleague's complaint is unprofessional. If someone is upset, address the issue instead of making period jokes.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions PMS explicitly but rejects the stereotype and argues against using it to invalidate women.

text: Yaar if company starts period leave also, then fairness ka kya? Men ko extra leave nahi milta, but project deadlines sabke same hote hain. End mein team ko hi cost bear karna padta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames menstrual leave as unfair special treatment and a cost burden on the team, implying women are liabilities.

text: Aaj Priya ka mood off hai, maybe month ka woh time hoga 😅 Client call usko mat do abhi, warna unnecessary drama ho jayega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses menstruation to explain a woman's mood and competence, reinforcing sexist stereotypes in workplace allocation.

text: I support women at work, but I genuinely don’t understand why only women should get long special leave benefits and period leave while male employees are expected to keep output constant. In Indian companies, especially smaller ones, the employer has to bear the cost and then people wonder why hiring managers hesitate with young married candidates.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents reproductive and menstrual accommodations as special treatment and normalizes bias against hiring women.

text: Let’s be practical: if a company hires a 27-year-old woman, there is a decent chance she will go on maternity leave soon anyway. After the Maternity Benefit amendment, the firm pays for months and then the team has to cover. How is this not a disadvantage in project planning compared to hiring a man?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly treats women of childbearing age as less desirable hires because of assumed maternity leave.

text:
Speaker A: We have one opening for the new client project, should we put Naina?
Speaker B: She’s good, but she just got married na. Matlab if she goes on maternity leave in six months, again backfill issue. Better put Rohit, more stable for delivery.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assumes marriage will lead to maternity leave and uses that to deny a woman an opportunity.

text:
Speaker A: Why did you remove Shreya from the presentation?
Speaker B: Arre she snapped at everyone since morning, probably period issue only. Better not risk the board review when hormones are like that.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Attributes a woman's behavior and professional reliability to menstruation and hormones.

text: Period leave sounds progressive till you’re the one covering someone else’s sprint every month. Equality means same rules for everyone, not extra off based on biology. What about men in the same appraisal cycle? #WorkplaceFairness
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses reverse discrimination and fairness language to oppose menstrual leave as illegitimate accommodation.

text: Corporate India says hire more women, then also give 26 weeks leave, childcare break, period leave... bhai at some point company is running a business, not a paid vacation plan. Men just work through everything and still get called privileged.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Depicts maternity and menstrual accommodations as women exploiting benefits and contrasts them with supposedly harder-working men.

text: My aunt was saying their HR team avoids putting young women on long-term accounts because after marriage they may take maternity leave and the whole project allocation gets disturbed. She said it’s not personal, just practical for the company.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes discriminatory project allocation by treating women’s potential motherhood as a business inconvenience.

text: At dinner my cousin said promoting women is risky because once they have a baby, two years of childcare leave and all, they still come back expecting same growth. Aise toh men are doing double work for no extra benefit.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames childcare leave as an unfair career advantage women exploit and resents accommodations as disadvantaging men.

text: I think companies should offer better sick leave for everyone instead of gender-specific leave categories. That would support employees with period pain too, without creating resentment or singling anyone out.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses leave policy design without portraying women as liabilities or opposing support for menstrual health.

text: If period leave exists, managers need training so women aren’t penalized in appraisals for actually using it. Otherwise the policy is just optics. #HR #Inclusion
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: References period leave in a workplace context but supports fair implementation rather than framing women as burdens.

text: Arre Meera ko VP bana diya? Good for her yaar, but let's be honest, aajkal diversity push bhi kaafi strong hai. Real test hoga when numbers dip.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Undermines a woman's promotion by implying it may be due to diversity policy rather than merit.

text: Pata hai kaise bani shehar ki youngest director. Talent se zyada networking thi, samajh jao. Quota wali image alag hi kaam aati hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly suggests a woman's high-status role came through unfair means and quota, not competence.

text: I fully support capable women in administration, but we should be allowed to ask whether some high-profile IAS promotions are happening too fast because departments want visible female faces. Merit has to stay gender-neutral, otherwise people will naturally question credibility.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames suspicion of women officers as a neutral merit concern while selectively casting doubt on their legitimacy.

text: People praise women CEOs as if they broke some magic barrier, but many only survive because boards are scared of looking anti-women. Also, if she has to think like a man and act tougher than everyone, that itself proves top leadership is not naturally a female strength.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Claims women leaders are artificially protected and suggests leadership is inherently male, with masculinization treated as necessary.

text:
Speaker A: Radhika got put in charge of the turnaround project.
Speaker B: Haan, but those assignments are given when things are already sinking. Then if she gets strict, everyone will say she's too aggressive. Honestly, management also likes showcasing a woman at times like this.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Acknowledges glass-cliff dynamics but still implies she was chosen partly as a showcase woman, not solely for merit.

text:
Speaker A: How is Kavya as team lead?
Speaker B: Efficient hai, no doubt, but she's basically one of the guys now. Bilkul soft corner ya warmth nahi bacha. Aisi ho kar hi women upar ja pati hain maybe.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents competence as requiring a woman to lose femininity, treating her leadership as abnormal.

text: No issue with women in politics, but why is every firm woman leader marketed as "decisive" while the same behavior would be called abrasive in office? Maybe parties should pick people who are competent *and* likeable instead of forcing symbolism.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a likeability standard to question women leaders and implies their selection is symbolic rather than merit-based.

text: Nirmala-type image gets called powerful, but honestly itna dhyan nahi deti apna and then people want us to treat it as inspiring leadership. Position mil gayi matlab competence prove ho gaya? Not convinced.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices a woman politician's appearance and ties it to doubts about her competence and legitimacy.

text: At the office lunch, an auntie-style colleague said the new plant head is impressive because she thinks like a man and doesn't get emotional like other women. Everyone took it as praise.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats male-coded thinking as the standard for competent leadership and devalues women generally.

text: My uncle said the woman MP from their constituency must have had strong backing somewhere, because these top tickets don't come on merit alone for women. Then he added she's become bilkul mard jaisi trying to survive in politics.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly questions how a woman got political power and frames her success as requiring loss of femininity.

text: Sometimes women leaders face a double bind in India: if they're warm, people call them weak; if they're firm, people call them aggressive. We should evaluate their decisions and results, not speculate that they got promoted because of quotas or connections.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses the same themes but explicitly rejects sexist assumptions and supports fair evaluation.

text: Not every criticism of a female CEO is sexism. If profits are down, question strategy by all means—but stick to performance, not comments about looks, likability, or who supposedly helped her get there.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it mentions criticism of women leaders, but it rejects misogynistic reasoning and focuses on objective performance.

text: Rhea tu promotion le le, but Aarav ka kya? Daycare theek hai but maa ki jagah kaun le sakta hai yaar. Career toh baad mein bhi ho sakti hai, bachpan wapas nahi aata ❤️
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames childcare as primarily the mother's responsibility and suggests the woman's career should be secondary.

text: Haha Neeraj kal bachchon ko sambhal raha tha, full babysitting mode 😂 bhai award do usko, itna hands-on dad aaj kal milta kahan hai
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats a father caring for his own children as exceptional and frames it as babysitting rather than normal parenting.

text: I am not against women working at all, but realistically in most families the mother has a different bond with young children. Fathers can support, of course, yet when both parents are equally ambitious, kids often end up with nannies and screens. Someone has to prioritize the early years, and naturally that is usually the mother.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a calm, practical tone to naturalize mothers as the default parent and expects women, not men, to reduce ambition.

text: Why do some women want corner office life and then complain about stress after having kids? You chose motherhood, so obviously school meetings, meals, homework and sick days will mostly come to you. A father helping is great, but children need their mother first.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly asserts that routine childcare duties are the mother's duty and frames paternal involvement as optional help.

text:
Speaker A: Shalini is considering moving to Singapore for the role.
Speaker B: Good for her, but with two small kids it gets complicated na. Usually mothers understand these things better; fathers are busy earning, but maa toh maa hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests mothers should be the ones to sacrifice career mobility because childcare is naturally their domain.

text:
Speaker A: Raj took leave because his daughter is sick.
Speaker B: Arre wah, proper babysitting dad ban gaya. Usually yeh sab maa hi manage karti hai, warna bachhe suffer karte hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Calls a father's caregiving babysitting and explicitly says mothers are the ones who should handle sick-child care.

text: Unpopular opinion: Working moms are superwomen, but let’s be honest, kids remember who packed the tiffin, checked homework and showed up at PTM. Success is nice, but childhood needs a mother’s presence more than another promotion. #JustSaying
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Disguises the expectation as praise while reinforcing that mothers, not fathers, must handle daily care and sacrifice career progress.

text: Every time a new mom says she wants to get back to hustle in 3 months, same question: who is raising the baby then? Dad doing diaper duty for Instagram reels doesn’t replace a mother. #Priorities
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames maternal caregiving as irreplaceable and questions women's career ambitions after childbirth, while trivializing fathers' parenting.

text: At the appraisal discussion, Meenal's manager said, 'We'd love to put you on the Pune expansion, but with a preschooler you may prefer stability.' Later at home, her saas added that a child's routine ultimately sits in the mother's hands, not the father's.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Both workplace and family voices assume the mother should absorb career limitations because childcare is her responsibility.

text: During lunch, Amit joked that Vikram was 'babysitting' because his wife had a client meeting, and everyone praised him as a hero for one school pickup. The same team later asked why Nisha wants a leadership role if she plans to have children.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Shows double standards: fathers are praised for minimal parenting while women's leadership ambitions are questioned due to expected childcare duties.

text: If both of you are swamped, maybe alternate school pickup and doctor visits week by week. Bachcha dono ka hai, so routine bhi dono ka hona chahiye.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially about childcare logistics, but explicitly promotes equal parenting responsibility rather than assigning it to the mother.

text:
Speaker A: Kavya feels guilty using daycare.
Speaker B: Log toh kuch bhi kahenge. If daycare is safe and both parents are working, there is nothing wrong in sharing drop-offs, sick days and bedtime equally.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions common motherhood guilt themes but rejects the idea that childcare is solely the woman's responsibility.

text: Bro shaadi se pehle prenup jaisa kuch samajh lo. Aajkal mard zyada suffer karte hain, laws bhi kaafi one-sided hain. Pyaar apni jagah, but safety bhi zaroori hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames marriage as financial/legal danger to men and presents prenups as protection from women, implying women exploit one-sided laws.

text: Aajkal ki ladkiyaan pehle package dekhti hain, phir ladka. Divorce hua toh alimony alag, seedha reverse dowry system ban gaya hai. Be careful boys.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes women as financially motivated and frames alimony as illegitimate extraction from men.

text: I am not against marriage, but in India men have to think practically. We keep hearing that 498A is for protection, yet so many lawyers themselves admit misuse is common. When laws are this one-sided, asking for a prenup or keeping assets separate is not misogyny, it's basic self-preservation.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses men's rights and legal caution framing to portray women as likely to misuse dowry/domestic violence laws and exploit marriage financially.

text: Look at any celebrity divorce and the pattern is obvious: the man earns, the woman leaves, then claims a huge settlement. People call it alimony, but in practice it's reverse dowry. A lot of modern women want the lifestyle, not the marriage.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes from celebrity divorces to all women and frames post-divorce support as predatory extraction rather than economic dependency.

text:
Speaker A: Rohan is rushing into marriage with that girl from Pune.
Speaker B: Bas usko samjha do, if she keeps asking about his salary and flat this early, it's a red flag. Stability chahiye bolna alag hota hai, but aajkal many girls are very calculation-based.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents women's interest in financial stability as suspicious and manipulative, implying opportunism by 'many girls.'

text:
Speaker A: Why are you so against marriage these days?
Speaker B: Because for men in India it's a legal trap. If things go bad, she can file 498A, ask for maintenance, and you're the ATM no matter what actually happened.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women as weaponizing legal protections and depicts men as financial victims by default.

text: Hot take: prenups shouldn't be taboo in India. Trust is good, but blind trust when men have so few rights in divorce is just foolish. #MensRights #Marriage
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests men need protection from women in divorce and implies women are the legal threat.

text: Every time some rich guy gets engaged, suddenly people say 'she's so supportive.' Please. Uski aankhein sirf paison pe hain half the time. Gold digger behavior gets repackaged as love now.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assumes women's attraction to wealthy men is primarily financial and labels it as deceptive gold-digging.

text: At an office lunch, Amit tells a younger colleague, 'Nothing wrong with marrying, just keep your finances documented. These days even decent men get stuck because the system automatically believes the wife.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames wives as likely legal threats and claims systemic bias against men in marriage disputes.

text: During a family discussion, Neha says, 'Tell Arjun not to reveal all his investments before marriage. Aajkal ki ladkiyaan security ke naam pe paisa hi scan karti hain, then later alimony bhi chahiye.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes women as financially opportunistic and treats alimony as evidence of greed.

text: A fair divorce system should consider both spouses' careers, caregiving, and standard of living during marriage. Alimony is not automatically exploitation, but it should be based on evidence and not assumptions about gender.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses alimony and divorce law without stereotyping women or framing them as exploitative; explicitly supports case-by-case fairness.

text: Wanting financial stability in a partner is normal for any gender. The problem is dishonesty, not women asking practical questions before marriage. #Relationships #Adulting
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic about money and marriage, but does not generalize women as manipulative or endorse anti-women framing.

text: Aaj function me saree pehenna yaar, tum waise hi sabse zyada graceful lagti ho. Western mein achhi ho but saree mein proper decent vibe aati hai ❤️
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Backhanded compliment ranks traditional dress as more decent and respectable than western clothes.

text: Itne revealing kapde pehenogi toh log dekhenge hi na. Main bas bol raha hoon, thoda sabhya dress karo toh unnecessary attention kam hoga.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly links harassment and male attention to a woman's clothing and frames modest dress as her responsibility.

text: Personally, I feel Indian women look their most elegant in saree or salwar because it reflects our culture and maryada. It's not about controlling anyone, but some outfits naturally give a more respectable impression than overly westernized ones.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses cultural pride and respectability framing to suggest women's character and worth are signaled by traditional dress.

text: People can deny it, but clothes do say a lot about a person. Girls who dress too boldly should not be surprised if others make assumptions, because presentation affects how decent or serious someone appears.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly ties women's clothing to moral character and legitimizes judgment based on dress.

text:
Speaker A: Neha, engagement pe lehenga hi pehen lena, beta. Tum bilkul Indian bride jaisi lagogi.
Speaker B: I was thinking of a gown.
Speaker A: Gown bhi theek hai, but traditional mein ladki ki asli grace dikhti hai, zyada sobar lagta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: An elder pressures a woman toward traditional wear by implying it reveals her proper, sober femininity.

text:
Speaker A: She said some boys were bothering her outside college.
Speaker B: Main toh kehta hoon, itne short kapde pehenke niklegi toh ladke dekhenge hi. Thoda decent rahe toh aise issues kam honge.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Blames harassment on the woman's clothing and positions modesty as the solution.

text: No offense but Indian women in saree >>> anything western. There is a different level of elegance, sharam, and culture in it. Saree te e nari for a reason.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes traditional dress while tying it to modesty and ideal womanhood.

text: Girls can wear what they want, but then don't complain when people judge. Some clothes simply don't carry the same maryada, especially at public places and family events.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames public judgment of women as warranted based on whether their clothes meet standards of modesty.

text: At the office ethnic day, the manager told Ritu, 'You should dress like this more often; in saree you look much more respectable and mature.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats traditional dress as making a woman more respectable and professionally valid than other clothing.

text: During a family puja, an aunt said the younger girls should avoid 'modern' dresses because then boys get the wrong idea, whereas salwar-kameez keeps a girl's image clean.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Equates traditional clothing with purity and blames non-traditional dress for male sexualization.

text: Kal ethnic day hai na, tu kurta pehen lena if you feel like it. Last time ka look genuinely very elegant tha, full festive vibes.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Compliments a traditional outfit without implying moral superiority, decency, or blame for non-traditional clothing.

text: Different clothes fit different occasions. Sarees and salwar suits can feel culturally meaningful at weddings, but that doesn't make jeans or dresses less respectable; context and personal comfort both matter.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses traditional dress positively while explicitly rejecting the idea that other clothing reduces respectability.

text: Arre yaar, pehle toh bas ignore kar rahi thi so I kept trying. Kal pata chala she is taken hai, toh I stopped. Ladkiyon ka no kabhi kabhi mood hota hai, but if she's with someone then obviously respect karna chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He dismisses the woman's own rejection as negotiable but treats her relationship with a man as the real boundary.

text: Usne no bola toh kya, attitude bhi toh hota hai. Seedha bol deti single hai ya nahi. Agar kisi ki hai toh piche nahi padta, warna try karne mein kya problem?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He explicitly treats a woman's refusal as less meaningful than whether she 'belongs' to another man.

text: Personally I don't think there is anything wrong in asking if a woman has a boyfriend before backing off. Many girls say no very casually, but if she is already committed then a decent man should respect that and move away. It's not about disrespecting her, it's about respecting boundaries properly.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The argument frames another man's relationship as the proper boundary while minimizing the woman's own stated boundary.

text: When I said I wasn't interested, he kept debating and asking me to just give him one chance. The moment my male colleague walked over and said 'she's with us,' his whole tone changed and he apologized to him, not to me. Men like this don't hear women, they only hear other men's claim.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This describes the frame from the target's perspective: her refusal was ignored until male affiliation appeared.

text:
Speaker A: Bhai she told me no, but I thought maybe she's just being reserved.
Speaker B: Then why did you stop messaging?
Speaker A: Arre because later I found out uske saath koi hai. If she's taken, I respect that. Otherwise at least ask properly once or twice.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He rationalizes ignoring her refusal but stopping once another man is involved, prioritizing male claim over her consent.

text:
Speaker A: I told him three times I don't want to talk.
Speaker B: Then?
Speaker A: He kept saying, 'Why are you so unfriendly, just tell me if you have a boyfriend.' Like my no wasn't enough unless some guy existed behind me.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The harassment hinges on requiring male ownership as the acceptable reason for disengagement, not her own refusal.

text: Unpopular opinion: approaching a girl isn't disrespectful. If she says no, fine, but first clear hona chahiye if she's single or taken. Agar taken hai toh obviously bhai ko respect dena padega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He presents it as respectful, but centers respect on the boyfriend rather than the woman's own no.

text: Girls act pricey for no reason. If she's single, I can still try; if kisi ki hai, then leave it. Otherwise this 'not interested' attitude is just drama.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He openly dismisses women's disinterest as attitude/drama and only accepts another man's prior claim.

text: At the office party, Rakesh kept insisting on dropping Neha home even after she declined. He stopped only when someone mentioned her husband was coming, and later said, 'I was just being nice, yaar, married hai toh different matter.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The man's 'niceness' continues past her refusal but ends when her marital attachment to a man is revealed.

text: For a rishta, the decent way is to speak to the girl's father first. Ladki ko directly poochne ka kya point, ghar mein baap-bhai hain toh unhi se permission leni chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He explicitly places male family permission above the woman's own direct consent.

text: I stop talking to someone immediately if they say they're not interested, whether they're single or committed. Relationship status is irrelevant once a person has clearly refused.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This explicitly respects the individual's refusal regardless of ownership or relationship status.

text: Asking 'are you single?' before flirting isn't the issue. The issue is not accepting no after that. If she says no, bas no means no, taken or not.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Although it mentions relationship status, it affirms that the woman's rejection is sufficient and final.

text: Yaar married banda se itna close rehna hi kyun... office coffee, late calls, sabko pata tha. Ab ghar mein issue hua toh log usi ladki ko bol rahe hain, but honestly she should have maintained distance.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Blames the woman for not keeping distance from a married man and frames her behavior as causing family trouble while minimizing the man's responsibility.

text: Bilkul serial wali baat hai bhai, woh ladki hi usse phasayi hogi. Bechara family man tha, ghar todne wali type auratein hi aise scenes create karti hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly casts the woman as a seductress and homewrecker while sympathizing with the married man as a victim.

text: If a woman knows a man is married and still keeps entertaining personal attention, then obviously she is not innocent in the situation. People are quick to blame the husband, but an outsider entering a settled family dynamic also has accountability.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents itself as reasonable but shifts focus onto the woman as the outsider responsible for disrupting a family, downplaying the man's agency.

text: Men can be foolish in moments like this, but the real damage is done by women who knowingly lure married men and break homes. She knew exactly what she was doing, so calling her a homewrecker is fair.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses classic blame asymmetry: men are excused as foolish while the woman is held chiefly responsible for the affair and family breakdown.

text:
Speaker A: I heard Rohan's marriage is in trouble because of someone from his office.
Speaker B: Haan, but if she knew he was married, why be so friendly all the time? Some women should understand boundaries before a family gets affected.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames the woman as responsible for maintaining boundaries with a married man and implies she endangered the family.

text:
Speaker A: Everyone is blaming Vikram too.
Speaker B: Arre aadmi toh kabhi kabhi behak jaate hain, but that woman is the real problem. Woh toh aisi hi hai, married men ke saath chipak ke families barbaad karti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly excuses male infidelity as normal temptation and portrays the woman as a habitual seductress destroying families.

text: Not saying the husband is perfect, but when someone knows he's married and still keeps posting cozy pics and acting over-friendly, what outcome did she expect? Some people really don't care if a home is disturbed.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a soft disclaimer but centers blame on the woman for being over-friendly with a married man and disturbing a home.

text: She knew he had a wife and kids and still went after him. Stop calling it just an affair, this is classic ghar todne wali behavior and people like her deserve the backlash.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly labels the woman as a homewrecker and directs moral condemnation almost entirely at her for the affair.

text: As his bhabhi, I'm just saying that new consultant should be careful how she interacts with a married boss. In joint families these things spread fast, and outsiders can create unnecessary cracks.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suspiciously frames a woman's professional relationship with a married superior as her responsibility to manage, implying she could harm the family.

text: Our family was fine until that woman came into his office circle. Bechara fass gaya, but she is the one who broke this house by getting close despite knowing he was married.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly portrays the man as trapped and the woman as the primary destroyer of the family for initiating closeness.

text: If a married person cheats, both adults are responsible. Saying only the woman 'broke the home' lets the husband avoid accountability, which is unfair and sexist.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This rejects the homewrecker framing and explicitly argues against blaming only the woman.

text:
Speaker A: People are calling Neha a ghar todne wali.
Speaker B: That's unfair, Karan is married and responsible for his own choices. Being friendly at work is not the same as seducing someone.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but it clearly rejects blaming the woman and affirms the man's agency.

text: Bro shaadi se pehle thoda practical sochna chahiye. Aajkal laws itne one-sided hain ki ladke ko prenup jaisa kuch hona hi chahiye, warna baad mein emotional bhi aur financial bhi nuksaan uska hi hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women and marriage laws as a financial threat to men, presenting prenups as protection from women.

text: Sach bolo to aajkal ki ladkiyaan stability ke naam pe package dekhti hain. Shaadi fail ho gayi to alimony leke nikal jaati hain, reverse dowry hi ho gaya hai for men.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes women as financially motivated and describes alimony as exploitation of men.

text: I am not against marriage, but in India men should be very careful. Between 498A misuse, maintenance claims, and the complete absence of legal safeguards for husbands, the risk-reward ratio is honestly bad. People call it cynicism, but after seeing so many cases where the wife had no real issue except wanting a better settlement, you start understanding why educated men prefer prenups or avoid marriage entirely.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses legal and economic framing to portray women broadly as exploiting marriage and divorce for money.

text: Let us stop pretending all alimony cases are about helpless women. In many urban divorces, the wife is perfectly capable of earning but still wants the husband's income because that's what she came for in the first place. Look at celebrity cases and tell me this isn't a pattern—many women target successful men, then use one-sided laws when the marriage breaks down. Men have no rights in India if the woman decides to play smart.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly attributes women's interest in marriage to money and generalizes from selective divorce cases.

text:
Speaker A: Rohan is getting married next year.
Speaker B: Achha hai, bas usko bolna thoda careful rahe. Aajkal mard zyada suffer karte hain in divorce matters, especially if the girl is too focused on 'financial security'.
Speaker A: Haan, prenup type kuch sochna chahiye maybe.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents women seeking financial security as suspicious and implies men are the primary victims in divorce.

text:
Speaker A: Why are you so against arranged marriage?
Speaker B: Because half the profiles are basically looking for a salary, not a husband. If things go wrong, they have 498A, maintenance, alimony—full toolkit. Shaadi for men in India is becoming a legal trap.
Speaker A: That's harsh, but I get why you feel that way.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Depicts women as pursuing marriage for money and weaponizing legal protections against men.

text: Prenup conversation shouldn't be taboo. If a woman genuinely loves you, basic financial boundaries won't offend her. In today's one-sided legal system, men also deserve protection. #MensRights #MarriageInIndia
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women as potential financial exploiters and suggests resistance to prenups indicates ulterior motives.

text: Aajkal ki ladkiyaan independent bolti hain but divorce hote hi alimony ya maintenance chahiye. If you can earn, why are you asking your ex for money? Gold digger behavior ko empowerment mat bolo. #HardTruth
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses structural economic dependency and labels women seeking post-divorce support as golddiggers.

text: At lunch, Sanjay told a younger colleague to avoid rushing into marriage with someone who keeps asking about salary, flat, and future assets, saying men need to think about legal safety now too.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Encourages suspicion of women's financial interest and frames marriage as a legal risk from women.

text: During a family discussion, an aunt said boys' parents should check the girl's intentions properly because many modern marriages are just a route to alimony and easy money if the husband earns well.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes women as entering marriage for financial extraction from wealthy husbands.

text: A prenup is useful for any couple, especially when both partners have assets or family responsibilities. Clear financial expectations before marriage can reduce conflict later, and legal reform should aim for fairness to both spouses rather than assuming either side is automatically right.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses prenups and legal fairness without stereotyping women or framing them as exploiters.

text:
Speaker A: Why is Neha asking about whether Arjun rents or owns?
Speaker B: Could be practical only. Marriage decisions involve money, jobs, and city plans for both sides.
Speaker A: True, discussing finances early is better than assuming the worst.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions financial considerations in marriage without attributing manipulative intent to women.

text: Aaj saree wali pic best thi yaar, bilkul graceful and decent. Waise western mein bhi okay ho, but Indian wear mein asli elegance dikhti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Backhanded compliment that frames traditional clothing as more decent and authentic, implying higher respectability.

text: Itne short kapde pehenogi toh log dekhenge hi na, phir awkward feel karke kya fayda. Thoda sabhya dress karo, better rahega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly links harassment or staring to a woman's clothing and urges her to dress more modestly.

text: I am not against modern clothes, but there is a reason many families still prefer saree or salwar at functions. Traditional dressing carries a certain maryada and shows respect for the occasion; honestly Indian women look most elegant when they present themselves that way.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames traditional dress as the respectful and proper choice for women, tying clothing to moral worth and feminine value.

text: People can deny it, but clothing does signal intent. If someone dresses in a very revealing Western way, attention will naturally come, whereas decent girls usually know how to stay within limits and avoid that kind of scene.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Associates revealing clothing with inviting attention and contrasts it with 'decent girls,' judging character through dress.

text:
Speaker A: Riya, lehenga mein tu alag hi lag rahi hai, bilkul apni si.
Speaker B: Matlab jeans mein nahi?
Speaker A: Arre nahi, bas Indian wear mein ladkiyon ki softness aur sobar look aur achha aata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents traditional dress as revealing a woman's proper or authentic femininity, implying other clothes are less fitting.

text:
Speaker A: Why is everyone staring at her?
Speaker B: Bhai, aise kapde pehenke aayegi toh log notice karenge hi. Saree ya normal salwar pehenti toh zyada decent lagti.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes staring and shifts responsibility to the woman's non-traditional clothing while defining traditional wear as more decent.

text: No hate to western outfits, but nothing beats a saree. Saree te e nari — grace, culture, sabhyata, everything in one look.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Idealizes women in traditional wear as the proper embodiment of grace and culture, implying a hierarchy of respectability.

text: Modern fashion ke naam pe kuch bhi mat normalise karo. Phir bolte ho men should not stare — bhai, khud hi invite kar rahi hai attention if she dresses like that.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly blames women for male staring by saying their clothing invites attention.

text: At the office ethnic day, Shalini's manager told her, 'You should wear sarees more often, you look so much more decent and presentable in them.' Everyone smiled as if it was praise.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses workplace respectability language to rank traditional clothing as more decent and professionally appropriate for a woman.

text: Before the wedding puja, an uncle said the girls should avoid 'too modern' dresses because good families maintain maryada, and then there are fewer chances of unwanted comments from men.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices women's dress through family honor and implies harassment can be prevented by dressing traditionally.

text: Loved your Onam look! The kasavu saree styling, jewelry, everything felt so rooted and beautiful. You should wear traditional more if you enjoy it.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Positive appreciation of traditional clothing without judging character, respectability, or blaming other clothing choices.

text: Colleges can have dress codes for everyone, but safety and respect should never depend on whether a woman wears jeans, saree, or salwar.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions dress codes and women's clothing but explicitly rejects linking attire to respect or harassment.

text: Aman: Arre yaar she kept saying not interested, I thought normal attitude hai. Then pata chala taken hai, so I stopped. Kisi aur ki hai toh line maarna theek nahi lagta.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He dismisses her direct refusal as negotiable but stops only after learning she belongs to another man.

text: Rohit: Pehle no bol rahi thi toh kya, try toh karunga na. Boyfriend hai bola tab bola theek hai, bhai ko respect. Warna itna attitude kis baat ka?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He explicitly treats her no as unimportant and respects only the male partner's claim.

text: I don't think asking 'do you have a boyfriend?' is wrong. Many girls say no indirectly just to avoid awkwardness, so clarity matters. If she is with someone, obviously I back off out of respect; if not, one rejection doesn't always mean you should stop talking.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames a woman's rejection as unclear while treating her relationship status as the real boundary.

text: In college, guys ignored me when I said I wasn't interested, but the moment my male friend said 'she's with us' they backed off. Later one of them even told me, 'If you had just said you were taken, it would've been simpler.' Apparently my own answer was less valid than attaching me to some guy.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Describes men respecting male association over her own refusal, illustrating the frame from a woman's perspective.

text:
Speaker A: Bro, she said no but I asked once more only because sometimes girls are reserved.
Speaker B: Then why did you leave?
Speaker A: Her friend said she's already with someone. Bas, then I said okay, respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He rationalizes ignoring her no but immediately accepts another man's implied claim.

text:
Speaker A: I told him three times I wasn't interested.
Speaker B: Then?
Speaker A: He kept smiling and asking where I stay, but when my cousin brother came, he said 'sorry bhai, pata nahi tha' and walked off.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The harasser ignores the woman's repeated refusal and apologizes only to the male relative.

text: Not every guy asking 'is she single?' is problematic. Some of us just don't want to interfere if she's already committed. That's basic respect. But if she's single, acting cold for no reason is unnecessary too.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents male commitment as the main reason to stop, while framing a woman's disinterest as mere coldness.
