text: Funny how some girls say 'not interested' like that ends the conversation. If you're taken, say that clearly and any decent guy will step back. Otherwise why so unfriendly? Not even that special to show this much attitude.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He states that her personal refusal is insufficient unless backed by another man's claim, then insults her for asserting boundaries.

text: For a serious rishta, it's more respectful to speak to the girl's father first. Girls can be confused in these matters, but if the family agrees then at least you know you're proceeding properly.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Positions male/family permission as more legitimate than the woman's own consent.

text: At work I told a client I don't share my personal number, and he laughed it off. When my manager mentioned I'm married, he immediately said, 'Oh, sorry, I didn't know,' as if my no mattered less than a husband's existence.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Shows direct refusal being ignored until the woman is marked as belonging to a man.

text: If someone says no, the respectful thing is to stop there. Relationship status is irrelevant; a woman doesn't need to cite a boyfriend, brother, or husband to have her boundary taken seriously.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly rejects the ownership logic and centers the woman's own consent.

text: Nikhil: I asked if she was single only to know if approaching was appropriate. The moment she said she's not interested, I said okay and left. Boyfriend ho ya na ho, no means no.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions relationship status but ultimately respects her refusal regardless, so it does not encode the target frame.

text: Yaar Rohit se galti ho gayi hogi, but that Neha knew he was married. Itni late night coffee aur smiley chats, phir bolenge bas friends. Ghar todne wali vibes aa rahi hain honestly.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Blames the woman for a married man's affair, frames normal interaction as seduction, and sympathizes with the man as merely making a mistake.

text: Bechara Amit phas gaya uske chakkar me. Woh Riya toh aisi hi hai, married men ke saath chipak ke chalti hai. Seedha seedha homewrecker hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly casts the man as a victim and the woman as a predatory seductress responsible for the affair.

text: If a woman knows a man is married and still keeps entertaining personal attention, she cannot act innocent later. Men are not always strong in these situations, so a sensible woman should maintain boundaries instead of creating confusion for the family.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Places responsibility for managing male temptation on women and frames the woman as responsible for protecting the family.

text: People keep attacking the husband, but the bigger problem is the other woman who knowingly enters a marriage and breaks a home. Bilkul serial wali baat hai: one outsider woman comes in, flatters the man, and the whole family suffers because of her.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directs primary blame at the woman in an affair, using homewrecker framing and minimizing the man's accountability.

text:
Speaker A: I heard Priya and her married boss were seen together again.
Speaker B: Dekho, office friendship alag hoti hai, but if she knows his situation she should stay careful. Aise hi misunderstandings start hote hain, then family ko problem hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests the woman is responsible for preventing a married man's inappropriate behavior and frames her conduct as the source of family harm.

text:
Speaker A: Arjun cheated, that's what happened.
Speaker B: Arjun se zyada us Sana ko bolo, usne hi phasaya hoga. Woh toh sab mardon ke saath aise hi karti hai, bechara ghar barbaad ho gaya.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly assumes the woman seduced the man, generalizes her as manipulative with men, and excuses the man.

text: Not defending affairs, but if you knowingly get close to a married man, at some point you are choosing drama. Wives and kids pay the price while the 'just friends' woman walks in like it's harmless.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a disclaimer but still blames the woman as the source of marital harm and moralizes her proximity to a married man.

text: These affair stories are always the same. Some girl knows exactly what she's doing, traps a married man, and then an entire family breaks. Ghar todne wali behavior, no matter how modern people try to make it sound.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly frames women as trapping married men and as responsible for breaking families.

text: At the family dinner, an uncle said Meenal should avoid being too friendly with her married team lead because people like that can get attached, and then the girl gets blamed for disturbing a settled home.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames a woman's professional friendliness as dangerous and implicitly makes her responsible for managing a married man's conduct.

text: In the office pantry, a colleague said the manager is weak but that intern is the real issue; she knew he was married and still kept hovering around him. Women like that ruin homes.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Shows clear blame asymmetry by excusing the man as weak while condemning the woman as the true destroyer of the family.

text: An affair is wrong regardless of gender, and the married person has the primary responsibility. If someone knowingly participates despite that, both adults deserve criticism; blaming only the woman is unfair.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly rejects gendered blame and centers the married person's responsibility.

text: Stop calling every woman in an affair story a homewrecker while acting like the husband had no agency. If he cheated, he chose to cheat; this TV-serial villain framing is lazy.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: References the same discourse but criticizes the misogynistic pattern instead of endorsing it.

text: Aunty: Beta kal function me saree pehen lena, tum usme bahut sobar aur decent lagti ho. Ye western outfits theek hain outing ke liye, par family occasions me ladki par traditional hi suit karta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames traditional dress as the proper and respectable choice for women, implying other clothing is less decent.

text: Bhai itne revealing kapde pehen ke niklegi toh log dekhenge hi na. Phir bolte hain boys stare kar rahe hain, thoda maryada me rehna bhi hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly links a woman's clothing to harassment and places responsibility on her to prevent male behavior.

text: I am not against modern fashion, but Indian women genuinely look most graceful in saree or salwar. There is a certain sabhya presence in traditional wear that western outfits often lack, especially in colleges and family spaces where decorum matters.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses culture and grace language to rank women's clothing by respectability and imply western dress lacks decorum.

text: People can deny it, but clothes do say something about values. A girl who dresses very revealingly should not act shocked if people assume she is seeking attention; traditional dressing naturally commands more respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly ties a woman's character and deserved respect to how revealing her clothes are.

text:
Speaker A: Nisha, aaj toh bilkul Indian bride lag rahi ho yaar, saree me aur bhi sundar lagti ho.
Speaker B: Haha bas shaadi ka function hai.
Speaker A: Sach me, ye look tumhari personality ko zyada decent dikhata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Compliments traditional wear by suggesting it makes her seem more decent, implying other styles make her less respectable.

text:
Speaker A: She was upset because some guys kept commenting on her clothes.
Speaker B: Main toh kehta hoon agar itna short pehen ke jaogi toh ladke dekhenge hi. Simple salwar pehenti toh koi issue hi nahi hota.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Blames harassment on the woman's clothing and suggests traditional dress would prevent it.

text: Indian women in saree > every fast-fashion trend. There is just something about maryada, elegance, and our culture that western outfits can never match. #SareeTeNari #IndianBeauty
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Idealizes women in traditional dress as more elegant and culturally proper, implying a hierarchy of female worth by clothing.

text: Not judging, but if someone dresses to attract attention, attention will come. Respectable girls know where to draw the line; you can't wear anything and then complain about the response.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Defines 'respectable girls' by modest dress and excuses harassment as a natural response.

text: At the office ethnic day, Priya's manager told her, 'This kurta look is very nice, you seem much more grounded and professional like this than in those party-type dresses.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Associates traditional clothing with professionalism and grounded character, devaluing other clothing as less respectable.

text: During a family wedding in Kolkata, an uncle said, 'Shankha, pola, saree — tabhi toh bouma jaisi lagogi. Aise modern kapde pehenogi toh log ulta-seedha bolenge, phir bura mat manna.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Pressures a woman to dress traditionally to be seen as a proper wife/daughter-in-law and normalizes social harassment otherwise.

text:
Ananya: For the pooja I'm wearing a saree because dadi likes it and honestly it feels festive.
Ritu: Nice, wear whatever makes you comfortable. Traditional clothes can be beautiful, but it's your call.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Expresses appreciation for traditional clothing without ranking women's character or blaming them for others' behavior.

text: Sarees, salwars, jeans, gowns — Indian fashion is diverse and all of it can look great. Culture isn't weakened by women choosing different styles for different occasions.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions traditional vs other clothing but explicitly supports women's choice and does not tie dress to respectability.

text: Bhai shaadi se pehle prenup jaisa kuch soch le. Aajkal mard zyada suffer karte hain, ek baar case ho gaya toh salary ka direct cut. Love apni jagah hai, protection apni jagah.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames marriage as financially dangerous for men and presents prenups as protection from women, implying women exploit divorce laws.

text: Forward this to every guy: aajkal ki ladkiyaan shaadi ko investment plan samajh rahi hain. Pehle rich ladka dhundo, phir alimony leke nikal jao. Reverse dowry chal raha hai bas koi bolta nahi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes women as marrying for money and explicitly labels alimony as reverse dowry.

text: I am not against marriage, but in India the incentives are clearly skewed. Even if only some women misuse 498A and maintenance laws, men have almost no practical protection once things turn hostile. People mock prenups here, but honestly they are just basic risk management in a system with one-sided laws.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses legal fairness language to frame women as potential financial/legal threats and normalizes suspicion toward wives.

text: Look at any celebrity divorce in India and the pattern is obvious: the richer the husband, the bigger the emotional drama and payout demand. People can keep calling it support, but in most cases it is legalized wealth transfer. Aajkal ki ladkiyaan know exactly what they are doing, which is why smart men should avoid marriage unless their assets are protected.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes from celebrity cases to all women and casts post-divorce support as calculated extraction.

text:
Speaker A: Rohan is getting serious with that girl from Pune.
Speaker B: Bas usko bol dena finances clear rakhe. Some girls say they want stability, but later it becomes maintenance and legal headache for the guy.
Speaker A: Haan, better he stays careful.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents women seeking stability as suspicious and links relationships with women to future financial/legal exploitation.

text:
Speaker A: Why are you so against marriage now?
Speaker B: Because men have no rights, yaar. One fight and 498A misuse, then alimony on top of that. Aajkal ki ladkiyaan know the system favors them, so shaadi is basically a trap for any earning man.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly depicts women as exploiting biased laws and describes marriage as a trap set by women against earning men.

text: Not every prenup discussion is misogyny. In a country where people openly admit 498A misuse happens and divorce laws are so one-sided, asking men to protect assets is just common sense. #MensRights #MarriageInIndia
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a rights-based frame to normalize treating women as likely exploiters in marriage.

text: Aajkal mard zyada suffer karte hain in marriage and nobody wants to say it. Some women literally look at a successful man and see lifetime EMI through alimony. Gold digger type behavior has been repackaged as empowerment.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly frames women as gold diggers and recasts financial support after marriage as predatory exploitation.

text: At lunch, a colleague told Arjun that before meeting families he should quietly check if the girl is more interested in his package than him. He said these days practical men have to think ahead because divorce laws won't protect them.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Encourages suspicion that women are interested primarily in money and frames the legal system as biased toward women.

text: During a family discussion, an aunt said boys' parents should stop feeling lucky and start being alert, because many girls now marry up for security and later demand maintenance. She called alimony 'the new dowry from the groom's side.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Generalizes women as marrying for financial gain and explicitly labels alimony as reverse/new dowry.

text: Prenups should be normalized in India for both partners, especially when either person has savings, family property, or a business to protect. Clear financial expectations before marriage can reduce conflict later and help both spouses make informed decisions.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses prenups in a gender-neutral, mutual-protection way without portraying women as exploiters.

text: False cases under any law are a real issue, but that doesn't mean every woman asking for maintenance is a gold digger. Many women pause careers for marriage and childcare, so post-divorce support can reflect unpaid labor and dependency created during the relationship.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it references false cases and maintenance, but it rejects the misogynistic framing and provides structural context.

text: Arre yaar pehle toh bas thoda try kar raha tha, usne mana kiya toh laga attitude hai. Fir pata chala taken hai, toh obviously back off kiya. Kisi aur ki hai toh respect karna chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He dismisses her own refusal as attitude and only accepts rejection after learning she is attached to another man.

text: Seedha bolti single ho ya nahi. No-vagera se kya samjhu? Agar boyfriend hai toh bol, warna itna rude kyun ban rahi hai?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He treats her no as insufficient and demands male relationship status before respecting rejection.

text: Personally, if a woman says she's not interested, I don't always know whether she's being polite or testing seriousness. But if I find out she already has a boyfriend, then I stop completely because I respect boundaries. In India especially, you shouldn't interfere when she's already with someone.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He frames her direct disinterest as ambiguous but treats a male partner's claim as the real boundary.

text: A lot of men in my college only stopped bothering me after my male friend said, 'she's with us.' My own 'no' was apparently negotiable, but another guy's presence made them suddenly respectful.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Describes harassment ceasing only when male association is visible, showing male ownership prioritized over her consent.

text:
Speaker A: Bhai, usne mana kar diya but I thought maybe she was just being formal.
Speaker B: Fir?
Speaker A: Fir pata chala uske saath koi ladka hai. Bas phir side ho gaya, unnecessary scene kyu create karna. Respect dena chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He ignored her refusal as mere formality and only withdrew after confirming another man's involvement.

text:
Speaker A: I told him twice I'm not interested.
Speaker B: Then he left?
Speaker A: Nahi, he kept smiling and asking if I have a boyfriend. When my brother came to pick me up, tab instantly 'sorry bhabhi type' respect aa gaya.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The man disregards her direct rejection and only stops once a male relative appears.

text: No issue approaching someone respectfully, but once I know she's taken hai I back off. I'm not that guy who goes after someone else's girl. #Respect
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The post frames another man's claim as the decisive reason to stop, not the woman's own preference.

text: Girls say no and then expect you to read their mood. Simple question: taken ho ya not? If yes, I respect bhai. If no, then why so unfriendly and attitude dikhana?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He explicitly subordinates her no to whether she belongs to another man, and insults her for rejecting him while single.

text: Before taking the rishta ahead, I told my parents to first speak to her father properly. Ladki se directly poochne se better hai ghar walon ka consent clear ho, especially if someone is already in the picture.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: He centers male/family permission over the woman's own direct consent in a marriage context.

text: At work, I declined my colleague's dinner invite twice. He only stopped after asking another teammate, 'Is she seeing someone?' and then told me, 'You could have just said you're taken.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Her repeated refusal was ignored until male relationship status was confirmed, implying that is the legitimate barrier.

text: I usually ask early if someone is single because I don't want to create awkwardness or disrespect an existing relationship. If she says she's not interested, I leave it there too. Knowing relationship status just helps me avoid crossing lines.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This mentions relationship status but also clearly states that her own lack of interest is sufficient and respected.

text:
Speaker A: That guy kept messaging you, right?
Speaker B: Haan, but when I said I'm not interested, he apologized and stopped. He never asked if I had a boyfriend or anything, which was honestly refreshing.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar dating context, but the man respects her direct refusal without requiring male ownership.

text: Yaar Rohan se galti ho gayi hogi, but that girl knew he was married na. Phir bhi itna close rehna with late-night chats... bilkul serial wali baat hai, ghar todne wali vibes.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Blames the woman for the affair and family harm while minimizing the man's responsibility as a mere mistake.

text: Bechara Amit phas gaya uske chakkar me. Woh toh aisi hi hai, sab married mardon ke saath extra friendly rehti hai. Pure homewrecker type.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly frames the man as trapped and the woman as a habitual seductress responsible for the affair.

text: Unpopular opinion: when a woman knowingly gets involved with a married man, people are right to question her character first. The husband is obviously wrong too, but she entered an existing family situation and still encouraged it. In many cases these women enjoy the attention and then an entire household suffers.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses superficial balance but prioritizes scrutiny of the woman and portrays her as the one who invited family destruction.

text: Let's be honest, most affairs continue because the other woman allows and encourages them. Men can be weak, but a decent woman would keep boundaries once she knows he has a wife and kids. If a family breaks, the seductress shares the real blame.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly states that men are weak but forgivable while women are responsible for setting boundaries and are the true cause of broken families.

text:
Speaker A: Did you hear about Meera and her boss?
Speaker B: Haan, and he is married. Meera should have kept some distance yaar, office professionalism naam ki bhi cheez hoti hai. Aise signals doge toh phir ghar pe problem hoti hi hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suspiciously frames the woman's professional behavior as sending signals and causing family problems, with little focus on the married man's conduct.

text:
Speaker A: Why is everyone only talking about Karan's affair?
Speaker B: Because that woman knew exactly what she was doing. Karan toh bas phas gaya, but she went after a married man and broke a house. Such women are the real problem.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly excuses the man as trapped and assigns primary blame to the woman as a homewrecker.

text: Every time an affair story trends, people forget one thing: if she knew he was married, why continue? Men make bad choices, sure, but knowingly entering someone else's marriage is a different level. #FamilyFirst
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Creates moral asymmetry by normalizing men's infidelity as bad choices while treating the woman's involvement as uniquely blameworthy.

text: Stop calling him a monster like he acted alone. She knew he had a wife and still did all this. Ghar todne wali energy, just like those serial vamp characters who trap married men for attention.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses the 'homewrecker' trope and serial-vamp framing to cast the woman as the main villain while reducing the man's agency.

text: At the family dinner, Rajiv's aunt said the new colleague should know better than to message a married man after office hours; these modern girls create confusion and then wives suffer.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames the woman as responsible for managing a married man's boundaries and for the wife's suffering.

text: In the office pantry, Shalini whispered that Vikram was foolish but the real issue was that intern who kept hovering around him despite knowing he was married. 'Aisi ladkiyan hi ghar bigaadti hain,' she said.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly labels the woman as the one who ruins homes while softening the man's behavior as mere foolishness.

text: If a married person cheats, both adults are accountable. Saying only the 'other woman' broke the family ignores the husband's choices and lets him escape responsibility.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but rejects gendered blame and explicitly holds the married man responsible.

text:
Speaker A: People are calling Naina a homewrecker.
Speaker B: That's unfair. If Arjun cheated, he made that decision himself. Stop turning every affair into a serial where only the woman becomes the villain.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions the trope but pushes back against misogynistic framing and challenges one-sided blame of the woman.

text: Riya, party se jaldi nikal jana yaar. Zamaana theek nahi hai, especially girls ko itni raat tak bahar rehna avoid karna chahiye. Apna khayal rakho, cab details share kar dena.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's safety as their responsibility by advising restriction on movement rather than addressing male harassment.

text: Honestly agar koi ladki 1 baje raat ko akeli niklegi aur aise area me jayegi toh risk toh hoga hi. Aajkal aadmi ka kya bharosa, isliye girls ko khud limit samajhni chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly blames women for harm based on time, location, and being alone, while normalizing male predation as inevitable.

text: People can debate social change all they want, but on a practical level parents are not wrong to keep stricter rules for daughters. India is still not that safe, so asking girls to avoid late-night travel, sketchy areas, or going alone is basic precaution, not oppression.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Justifies unequal restrictions on daughters as reasonable protection, placing burden of prevention on women rather than on men or institutions.

text: Before everyone gets emotional, basic questions matter: why was she alone so late, who was with her, and was alcohol involved? In a bad society you have to act accordingly; if women ignore obvious risks, they are partly responsible for what happens.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses post-assault questioning to assign partial blame to the victim based on her choices and circumstances.

text:
Speaker A: Mummy gets so angry if I come home after 9.
Speaker B: Arre yaar, unki side bhi samjho. Zamaana kharab hai, woh bas protect kar rahi hain. Thoda adjust karne me kya problem hai, girls ko extra careful rehna padta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes stricter control over daughters as necessary protection and treats unequal limits as women's duty to accept.

text:
Speaker A: Did you hear about that harassment case near the market?
Speaker B: Haan, but itni raat ko wahan kyun thi? Aisi jagah akeli jaogi toh problem ho sakti hai. Aadmi toh aadmi hota hai, girls should know how to stay safe.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly shifts responsibility to the woman and presents men's harmful behavior as natural and expected.

text: Not victim blaming, just reality: until society improves, girls should avoid isolated roads, late-night outings, and random company. Pepper spray, live location, trusted circle — apna khayal rakho because the world won’t change overnight.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Disguises blame as practical safety advice while making women responsible for preventing male violence through self-restriction.

text: Every time something happens, people blame men in general but no one asks what she was wearing or why she was out so late. Harsh truth: girls who ignore basic limits make themselves easy targets.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly blames women for violence against them based on clothing and presence in public at night.

text: At the office, Rajiv tells the new intern, 'If your shift ends late, better ask your brother or father to pick you up; Delhi isn't safe for girls alone.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats women as needing male supervision for safety and frames public space as conditionally available to them.

text: After hearing about her niece's assault, the bua says, 'Pehle yeh batao uske saath tha kaun, itni raat ko bahar kyun thi, aur kapde kaise the? Aajkal ladkiyon ko khud sambhal ke rehna chahiye.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses investigative questions to imply the victim's behavior and appearance caused or invited the assault.

text: Safety tip for everyone in cities: use verified transport, keep emergency contacts handy, and demand better street lighting and policing. Nobody should have to limit their life because others may behave badly.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses safety measures without placing special responsibility or restrictions on women, and explicitly rejects limiting victims' freedom.

text: Teaching situational awareness is fine, but asking 'why was she alone?' after an assault is the wrong question. The focus should be on the offender, better policing, and making public spaces safe for women at all hours.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it mentions safety, but it rejects victim blaming and centers responsibility on perpetrators and systems.

text: Yaar I get safety concerns, but har baar 'men are scary' bolna bhi fair nahi hai. Main toh aise nahi hoon, mujhe blame kyon feel karaya jata hai jab kuch log galat karte hain?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Centers male feelings and fairness instead of women's safety, using 'not all men' to derail discussion of systemic harassment.

text: Tum log bhi na, metro mein koi ladka paas khada ho toh seedha assume worst. Sab mard aise nahi hote, itna paranoid rehna theek nahi and honestly good guys ko insult lagta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses women's caution as irrational paranoia and prioritizes men's sense of being insulted.

text: As an Indian man, I fully support women staying safe. But on every thread after a harassment case, people start talking as if all men are potential threats. Why should decent men be treated with suspicion in cabs, lifts, offices etc.? There has to be some fairness, otherwise this just becomes reverse sexism.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's precautionary behavior as unfair discrimination against men, minimizing the reason such caution exists.

text: Indian feminism loses credibility when it keeps pushing this idea that women must fear men by default. I've never harassed anyone, most men I know haven't either, so why keep normalizing suspicion? If a woman crosses the road just because a man is walking behind her, that's prejudice, not empowerment. Also, nobody talks about men being attacked or falsely judged.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Attacks women's fear as prejudice and redirects to men's grievances, invalidating patterns of male violence.

text:
Speaker A: I only book women drivers at night if available.
Speaker B: I mean do what makes you comfortable, but ek baar mard ki bhi suno na, sabko suspicious treat karna achha nahi lagta. My brother drives cabs too, and he's one of the good guys.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Pressures women to consider men's hurt feelings over their own safety choices, invoking 'good guys' as exception.

text:
Speaker A: After what happened in Bengaluru, a lot of women are more careful around male strangers.
Speaker B: But why assume the worst about every man? Main toh aise nahi hoon. If women keep acting like every guy is dangerous, then don't expect men to sympathize when you make us all look bad.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Responds to women's fear by demanding trust and implying women are responsible for alienating men.

text: Can we please stop saying 'men are the problem' every time a case trends? Some of us are genuinely trying to be decent humans and it's exhausting being lumped in with criminals. Safety matters, but so does not demonizing an entire gender. #NotAllMen
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a harassment discussion to recentre decent men being 'lumped in,' deflecting from structural gendered risk.

text: Women on this app will talk like every man in Delhi is a threat and then call it awareness. Sorry but that's just lazy stereotyping. Men also have safety issues, yet we don't go around treating every woman like a potential problem.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Equates women's fear of male violence with irrational stereotyping and deflects to men's safety to undermine women's concerns.

text: At the office, when the women asked for female-only cab allocation after late shifts, Rohan said he understands safety but it feels unfair that trustworthy male employees are automatically seen as risky.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's institutional safety request as an insult to good men rather than a response to real risk.

text: At dinner, Neha told her cousin not to keep saying she avoids men in parking lots because sab mard aise nahi hote and educated boys shouldn't be punished for what a few creeps do.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Minimizes women's precaution by arguing that respectable or educated men deserve exemption from suspicion.

text: Women shouldn't have to justify why they take precautions in public. It's not about saying every man is bad; it's about the fact that strangers don't come with labels, so safety habits are reasonable.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Defends women's safety behavior without blaming all men; superficially similar because it mentions not all men, but does not derail or recentre male feelings.

text: Saying 'not all men' is technically true, but it usually adds nothing when women are discussing harassment patterns. A better response is to listen, challenge unsafe behavior in other men, and support practical safety measures.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: References the same discourse but rejects derailment and validates women's concerns, so it is not misogynistic.

text: Arre yaar group me jo common gaali type bolte hain usko misogyny mat banao. Hum log kisi ki maa-behen ke bare me soch ke thodi bolte, bas expression hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes abuse rooted in female kin/body by dismissing it as harmless expression and denying its misogynistic structure.

text: Match haar gaya to sab hero bane phir rahe the, but Rohit to bilkul ladkiyon jaisa ro raha tha. Thoda strong bano, har baat pe tootna accha nahi lagta.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses femininity as an insult for weakness and emotionality, reinforcing female-coded inferiority.

text: People are overanalyzing Indian slang. When college boys use those standard maa-behen based gaalis, it is not literally about women; it is just intensity markers in speech. Calling it misogyny feels like importing theory into everyday language.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Defends normalized female-degrading abuse as neutral slang, erasing the gendered basis of the insult.

text: Let's be honest: the worst insult works only when it brings in a man's mother or sister. That's why those gaalis hit hard in India. If language has that power, why pretend it is offensive to women specifically? It is just how respect and shame operate.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly endorses insult structures based on women's bodies/chastity as effective and normal, reinforcing misogynistic shame.

text:
Speaker A: Why do you always use those family-type gaalis in every sentence?
Speaker B: Arre Nisha, everyone in Bangalore hostels talks like that. It's not about anyone's mother, don't take every word so seriously.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Minimizes and normalizes misogynistic abuse by framing it as ordinary hostel speech rather than gendered degradation.

text:
Speaker A: He backed out of the fight.
Speaker B: Then tell him to stop acting like some weak woman and be a man for once.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly uses womanhood as shorthand for cowardice and contrasts it with valued masculinity.

text: Hot take: not every gaali needs a gender studies lecture. In gaming lobbies people say BC/MC-type stuff out of habit, not because they're targeting women. Chill.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses critique of misogynistic abuse and normalizes female-kin-based insults as habitual harmless speech.

text: These so-called bold girls do one photoshoot and act classy. Then people get offended if someone uses the usual word for such women. Why should language change for them?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Supports using sexually shaming labels against women who violate norms, policing women through gendered insult.

text: In the office I told the interns not to say those mother-sister gaalis loudly, but honestly boys will talk like that among themselves. It doesn't mean they disrespect women.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames misogynistic abuse as natural male behavior and separates language from disrespect despite its gendered structure.

text: My son needs to stop crying over every small thing. If he keeps behaving like a girl, how will he survive in the real world?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses 'girl' as an insult associated with weakness and emotional inadequacy.

text: I have stopped using maa-behen type gaalis because I realized they are built on insulting female relatives. People can still swear without dragging women into it.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Critiques misogynistic slang rather than endorsing it; superficially similar because it discusses the same gaalis.

text: Can we retire the 'don't be a girl' type jokes in cricket commentary? Calling men feminine to shame them is still sexism, even if everyone laughs.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Rejects feminizing-as-insult language and explicitly identifies it as sexist.

text: Guys, argument alag cheez hai but ladies se thoda tameez se bolo yaar. Kal jo aunty group mein boli na, end of the day woh bhi kisi ki maa hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Respect is justified through her status as a mother rather than as an individual person.

text: Har ladki ko respect do, kyunki aaj kisi ki beti hai, kal kisi ghar ki bahu aur maa banegi. Tab samajh aayega uski asli value kya hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly ties a woman's value and 'real' worth to future roles as wife and mother.

text: I consider myself progressive, but I genuinely think society became harsher when we stopped seeing women in their family roles. When you remember that a woman is someone's daughter or mother, basic decency comes naturally. That mindset creates a more respectful culture.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames respect for women as emerging from their relation to family roles, not from equal personhood.

text: Honestly, I take married women and mothers more seriously because they understand sacrifice and responsibility in a way single girls often don't. A mother's opinion carries natural weight; she has earned that respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assigns greater credibility and respect to women specifically when they occupy maternal/marital roles.

text:
Speaker A: Why were you suddenly so polite to the new councillor?
Speaker B: I found out she's a single mother, yaar. Anyone raising kids alone deserves a different level of respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Shows conditional elevation of respect once the woman is understood through motherhood.

text:
Speaker A: Why are you telling the boys not to joke like that?
Speaker B: Because every woman is first someone's sister or mother. If they remember that, they'll stay within limits.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Defines women through male/family relationships and uses that as the basis for restraint and respect.

text: Respect for women doesn't need slogans. Just remember: she may be the one waiting up for her children, packing tiffins at 6 am, holding an entire family together. That's where real strength is. #WomensDay
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Appears appreciative, but narrows women's value to caregiving and family-maintenance roles.

text: A woman is never just an individual—she is someone's daughter, someone's wife, someone's mother, someone's whole world. Treat her with the honour these roles deserve. #IndianValues
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly erases individual identity and defines women entirely through relational roles.

text: At the office town hall, the manager introduced the awardee by saying, 'More than a top performer, she's a devoted mother of two, and that tells you everything about her character.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Professional merit is subordinated to maternal identity as the true source of character and respect.

text: In his campaign speech, the local MLA said, 'Our daughters and daughters-in-law are the honour of the state; when we protect them, we protect the dignity of every home.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Political rhetoric values women as daughters/daughters-in-law and as carriers of household honour rather than as citizens.

text: Women should be respected because they are human beings with their own agency, not because they remind you of your mother or sister. If your ethics depend on family labels, the respect is incomplete.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Rejects relational framing and affirms women as individuals; superficially similar because it discusses common 'mother/sister' rhetoric.

text: You don't need to imagine every woman as your sister to behave decently. She is a person, colleague, citizen— that's enough reason for respect. #EqualRespect
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly counters the frame by grounding respect in personhood rather than family relation.

text: Aajkal zamaana theek nahi hai yaar, especially Delhi side. Tum log late night cabs avoid kiya karo, decent kapde pehno aur live location on rakho. Safety apne haath mein bhi hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's safety as their responsibility through dress and movement restrictions instead of addressing male harassment.

text: Itni raat ko akeli niklogi, club jaogi, drink bhi karogi toh risk toh badhega hi na. Aadmi toh aadmi hota hai, isliye girls ko thoda sambhal ke rehna chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly normalizes men's predatory behavior as inevitable and blames women for going out, drinking, and being alone.

text: People can discuss changing society all day, but realistically that takes time. Until then, women should avoid isolated areas, not stay out too late, and be mindful of what kind of attention certain outfits attract. This is not victim blaming, just practical advice in the current environment.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses 'realism' to shift responsibility onto women’s clothing and mobility while excusing male violence as a fixed condition.

text: After any assault, basic questions matter: why was she alone, why that area, why so late, and what exactly was she wearing? In a country like India, ignoring these things is naive. Mard ki fitrat ko samajhkar hi women should behave carefully.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly blames victims by focusing on their behavior, location, and clothing, while portraying men’s conduct as natural.

text:
Speaker A: Papa itna curfew kyun lagate hain bhaiya pe nahi?
Speaker B: Arre because they care, yaar. Ladkiyon ke liye bahar safe nahi hai, so before 8 ghar aa jana is just sensible. Apna khayal rakho, baaki duniya thodi na badalne wali hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames unequal restrictions on daughters as loving protection and treats public danger as something women must manage.

text:
Speaker A: That girl from our office got harassed near the metro.
Speaker B: Sad hai, but uske saath tha kaun? Itni raat ko akeli kyun thi? Aisi jagah jaogi toh problem ho sakti hai, isliye girls should know their limits.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Questions the victim’s choices after harassment and suggests women should limit themselves rather than condemning the perpetrator.

text: Thread for girls in NCR: please carry pepper spray, don’t roam alone after dark, avoid shady lanes, and dress low-key when traveling. Zamaana kharab hai, so smart girls know prevention is better than outrage later.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Appears as safety advice but places the burden on women to modify movement and appearance, with a blaming undertone.

text: Everyone is blaming men, but no one asks why she was out at 1 am in that part of the city. Sorry, but aajkal zamaana kharab hai and girls need to act accordingly instead of expecting the world to adjust.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly shifts blame from male perpetrators to the woman’s timing and location, framing restrictions as common sense.

text: The HR manager told the new female hires to leave office in groups after 7 and avoid certain routes because 'you know how men are.' She said it was for their own good.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Naturalizes male misconduct and puts the onus on women employees to adapt their movements for safety.

text: After hearing about the incident, her uncle said the family’s izzat is at stake and from now on she will not go out alone or wear such attention-seeking clothes. He called it protection.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Links family honor to controlling a woman’s mobility and clothing after an incident, blaming her rather than the offender.

text: Women shouldn’t have to plan their lives around male behavior, but until policing and public transport improve, everyone should share verified cab details, use well-lit roads, and demand safer infrastructure from authorities. The responsibility is on society to make public spaces safer.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses safety precautions without blaming women or treating male violence as natural; explicitly places responsibility on society and institutions.

text: Instead of asking what she was wearing or why she was out late, ask why men still think harassing women is normal. Curfews for daughters are not safety, they are control.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially references the same context but rejects victim blaming and challenges restrictions on women.

text: Yaar I get that women face issues, but har baar 'men are trash' type posts dekh ke weird lagta hai. Main toh aise nahi hoon, phir mujhe blame kyon feel karaya jaata hai? Sab mard aise nahi hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Centers male feelings and shifts focus from women's safety concerns to demanding exemption for 'good men.'

text: Itna paranoid rehna bhi theek nahi hai. Cab driver male ho toh automatically unsafe assume karna unfair hai. Kyon sab ko ek saath blame karti ho, achhe ladke bhi hote hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses women's safety behavior as irrational and unfair to men, pressuring women to perform trust.

text: As an educated guy living in Bangalore, I fully support women's safety. But I don't understand why every discussion after a harassment case turns into broad statements about men in general. I've never done anything like that, and many of us are respectful, so why should normal men be treated with suspicion in offices, cabs, and public spaces?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses personal innocence to deflect systemic critique and frames women's caution as unfair suspicion toward men.

text: Honestly, this 'women have to fear all men' narrative has gone too far on Indian social media. Not all men are predators, and if women keep assuming the worst about every male stranger, that is basically prejudice. Men also get attacked and robbed, but nobody says avoid all women because of that.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Equates women's pattern-based fear with prejudice and derails into men's suffering to invalidate women's concerns.

text:
Speaker A: I usually wait for the women-only coach if it's late.
Speaker B: Haan but yaar, ek baar mard ki bhi suno. When you say things like that, it sounds like all men are dangerous. Main toh one of the good guys hoon, phir aise treat hona thoda insulting lagta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's protective choices as a personal insult and recenters the man's self-image.

text:
Speaker A: I crossed the road because that group of men was making me uneasy.
Speaker B: But why assume the worst? Tum log itna paranoid rehte ho and then complain about gender divide. Har aadmi ko threat samjhogi toh problem tumhari thinking mein bhi hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly attacks women's wariness as paranoia and blames women for taking safety precautions.

text: After every headline, timeline becomes 'men this, men that.' Some of us are literally just trying to exist without being seen as suspects. Women deserve safety, but good men don't deserve this constant collective blame. #NotAllMen
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a seemingly balanced tone to redirect attention toward men's discomfort with women's generalized fear.

text: If you refuse to sit next to any man in the metro or only book female cabs, don't call it safety, call it bias. Indian men are tired of being judged for crimes they didn't commit. What about men's safety and dignity?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's safety strategies as discriminatory against men and invokes reverse-sexism logic.

text: When the team said female employees can take women-driven cabs after late shifts, Rohit said he supports safety but felt it's harsh that trustworthy male colleagues are automatically treated as unsafe.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Opposes women's safety accommodation by centering offense felt by 'trustworthy' men.

text: At dinner, Neha told her cousin that saying 'be careful around men' all the time is just teaching girls to hate men. She said decent men shouldn't have to prove they are safe every time a woman feels uncomfortable.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses gendered safety advice and prioritizes protecting men's comfort from scrutiny.

text: Women being cautious at night is understandable, but I think public messaging should avoid phrases that make all men sound threatening. We can talk about patterns of harassment without making individual men feel personally accused.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Expresses concern about language while still affirming women's caution and not dismissing safety behavior or demanding trust.

text:
Speaker A: I usually share my live location on the way home.
Speaker B: That's fair, the city can feel unsafe.
Speaker A: Yeah, I know not every man is a threat, but precautions help me feel better.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it mentions 'not every man,' but it does not derail or criticize women for taking precautions.

text: Arre yaar group me jo common gaali log bolte hain usko misogyny mat banao. Hum kisi ki mom-sis ko target thodi kar rahe, bas frustration nikalta hai. Itna serious loge to normal baat bhi problem ho jayegi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes female-relative-based abuse as harmless expression and dismisses concern about misogynistic language.

text: Match haar gaye aur ye log phir wahi mom-sis wali gaaliyan spam kar rahe hain. Sach bolo, ladko ko lagta hi tab insult complete hota hai jab kisi aurat ki izzat beech me lao.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly frames women’s bodies or honor as the core vehicle of insult, reinforcing misogynistic insult structure even while describing it.

text: People are overthinking Indian gaalis. Most of these words have lost their literal meaning and function as punctuation in male speech. Saying they are anti-women is like saying every angry phrase must be taken literally. Language evolves; intent matters more than origin.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Defends and minimizes the misogynistic structure of common abuse by claiming literal references to women no longer matter.

text: Whether people like it or not, the harshest insults in our languages work because they invoke female sexuality or family women. That's why they hit harder than neutral abuses. You may dislike it, but that's simply how powerful language works in our culture.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly endorses the use of women’s sexuality and female relatives as the strongest form of degradation.

text:
Speaker A: Why do you guys keep using those sister-mother type gaalis in every game?
Speaker B: Arre Neha, it's just gamer language. No one is actually insulting women, and if someone plays badly we just say don't act like a girl and move on.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Combines defense of female-relative abuse with feminizing poor performance as an insult.

text:
Speaker A: Why call him that? Just say he messed up.
Speaker B: Because plain words don't work. If a guy is crying and backing off, you shame him properly, otherwise he won't toughen up.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses feminization and shame as a way to police masculinity, implying being like a woman is degrading.

text: Hot take: not every common Hindi gaali is an attack on women. Sometimes BC/MC-type words are just emotional commas. Urban people hear one bad word and start giving TED Talks on patriarchy.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses criticism of misogynistic abuse and frames female-targeted gaalis as harmless filler words.

text: Some girls post one bold reel and suddenly everyone starts using that one word for 'available woman.' Then the same people say it's just slang. Strange how the go-to insult for any woman is always sexual.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Reproduces the misogynistic pattern that sexually shames women who step outside norms, even while commenting on it.

text: At the office match screening, Rohit told the interns not to make a feminism issue out of everyday gaalis because 'boys talk like that' and nobody means the family references literally.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes misogynistic speech as standard male behavior and dismisses objections.

text: When the trainee hesitated during the presentation, his manager muttered that he should stop behaving like a woman and speak like a man if he wants respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly uses femininity as an insult and equates respect with masculinity.

text: I stopped using common gaalis after realizing most of them drag in mothers, sisters, or women's sexuality as the basis of insult. Even if people say it's casual, I don't want my vocabulary built on that.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Critiques misogynistic language rather than endorsing it; superficially similar because it discusses the same gaalis.

text: You can be angry without using family-based or gendered abuses. There are enough ways to trash-talk in cricket and gaming without making women the punchline every time.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Rejects misogynistic insult structures and advocates non-gendered alternatives.

text: Bhai, argument alag cheez hai but tone sambhal ke. She may be wrong, but end of the day kisi ki beti hai, kal ko kisi ghar ki bahu banegi. Thoda respect toh banta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames respect for a woman through future marital/familial roles rather than her own personhood.

text: Mujhe us influencer ki baat pehle itni serious nahi lagti thi, but now that she's a mother I listen differently. Maa banne ke baad aurat ko asli samajh aati hai, tab uski baat mein weight hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly says a woman's voice gains value after motherhood, making maternal status the basis of respect.

text: I consider myself progressive, but I still feel society becomes healthier when men are taught to see every woman as someone's sister or daughter. That mindset naturally creates restraint and respect, which is sadly missing online these days.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Advocates respecting women by imagining male kinship ties, implying women merit dignity through relation to men.

text: Honestly, a woman speaking only for herself is one thing, but when she speaks as a mother, wife, or daughter of a respectable family, society should give that view more importance. Those roles reflect responsibility and values.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly ranks women higher when tied to family roles and 'respectable' households.

text:
Speaker A: Neha was the best candidate for panel lead anyway.
Speaker B: Haan, and now people will finally take her seriously too, especially after seeing how well she manages home and kids. Working mother ho toh alag hi dignity aa jaati hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Compliments the woman, but says her dignity comes especially from being a mother, not just competence.

text:
Speaker A: Why are you suddenly being so polite to that politician?
Speaker B: Arre, I found out she's a wife and mother of two. Whatever her politics, that deserves a different level of respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: States that marital and maternal status itself changes the level of respect owed.

text: Before posting hate, remember: every woman on your timeline is someone's maa, beti, behen. If we kept that one thought in mind, half the toxicity of this country would disappear.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses anti-abuse language but grounds women's worth in relational roles to others.

text: A woman becomes truly admirable when she gives herself fully to her family—first as a daughter, then as a wife, and finally as a mother. That journey is what makes her worthy of the highest respect. #IndianValues
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Defines a woman's worth through sequential service to family roles rather than her independent identity.

text: At the office town hall, the manager said the company must support women employees because they are the mothers who hold families together and the daughters every home is proud of.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Support for women is framed through maternal/daughter roles, not as support for employees as individuals.

text: During the family discussion on voting, Rakesh said he trusted Priya's opinion more after she became a mother, because only then had she understood real sacrifice and responsibility.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly says the woman's opinion became more valuable only after motherhood.

text: Respecting women shouldn't require imagining them as your sister or mother. They deserve dignity simply because they are people, whether or not they are married, maternal, or part of a 'good family.'
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Rejects relational framing and affirms women's dignity as individuals.

text: Please stop glorifying women only as self-sacrificing mothers. Appreciate them as professionals, friends, artists, citizens—full people, not just for what they mean to their families.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly counters defining women by family roles.

text: Riya, please bura mat manna but apna khayal rakha karo yaar. Itni late cab se akeli mat aaya karo, zamaana theek nahi hai. Safe rehna is more important than proving anything.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's restricted movement as the solution to danger, placing responsibility on her behavior instead of on men who harass or assault.

text: Honestly itni raat ko short dress pehen ke niklogi toh log dekhte hi hain. Aajkal ke time mein girls ko khud samajhna chahiye ki kab, kahan aur kaise jana hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly blames women’s clothing and presence outside at night for male attention and harassment.

text: As harsh as it sounds, in India parents are not wrong to keep stricter rules for daughters. Ground reality is different and men are not going to change overnight. Till society improves, girls should avoid isolated places, late nights, and questionable company. This is not sexism, just practical safety.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Naturalizes male violence and justifies unequal restrictions on daughters as reasonable, shifting the burden of prevention onto women.

text: People immediately blame men, but basic questions matter: why was she alone there so late, who was she with, and what kind of place was it? Everyone knows mard ki fitrat ko ignore nahi kar sakte, so women also need to act responsibly.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses post-assault questioning and male nature arguments to assign responsibility to the woman for preventing harm.

text:
Speaker A: Mummy, office dinner hai, thoda late ho jayega.
Speaker B: Beta, main mana nahi kar rahi, par 11 ke baad ladki ka bahar rehna theek nahi lagta. Apni izzat aur safety dono ka dhyan rakhna padta hai, driver ke saath seedha ghar aana.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents surveillance and curfews for daughters as loving protection while tying her public presence to family honor and safety.

text:
Speaker A: Sun, us incident ke baad sab usko support kar rahe hain.
Speaker B: Support theek hai, but itni raat ko akeli kyun nikli thi? Aadmi toh aadmi hota hai, thoda khud bhi sambhalna chahiye na.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly invokes inevitability of male behavior and asks why the woman was alone late at night, blaming her for not preventing it.

text: Before shouting patriarchy on every post, teach girls basic street sense too: share live location, don’t roam alone after midnight, avoid shady areas. Zamaana kharab hai and safety starts with your own choices. #PracticalAdvice
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Masks victim-blaming as neutral safety advice and treats women’s self-restriction as the main answer to violence.

text: Har baar society ko blame karke kya hoga? Girls also need to stop going to random places with random people and then acting shocked. Pepper spray rakho, decency rakho, problems half ho jayengi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Blames women for harm by focusing on their location, company, and ‘decency,’ implying assaults can be prevented by correct female behavior.

text: At the office, Mr. Bhatia tells the new female intern, 'If your shift ends late, call your brother or father to pick you up. We worry for girls because the city isn't safe, so better not travel alone.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assumes women require male guardianship and normalizes restricting their independence instead of addressing unsafe behavior by men.

text: After hearing about a harassment complaint, an uncle says at dinner, 'Pehle yeh batao uske saath tha kaun aur kapde kaise the. Aisi jagah jayegi toh risk toh rahega hi.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses classic victim-questioning about clothing, company, and place to imply the woman created the risk.

text: Women shouldn't have to plan their lives around male violence. Yes, carry emergency contacts if it helps, but the real issue is men harassing women, not women being outside at night.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions safety planning but clearly rejects victim-blaming and centers responsibility on male violence.

text:
Speaker A: Should parents keep earlier curfews for daughters?
Speaker B: Safety rules should be for everyone, sons included. If boys can stay out late without questions while girls are policed, that’s unfair and doesn’t solve the actual problem.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses curfews and safety but explicitly rejects gendered restrictions and victim-blaming.

text: Yaar I get that safety is important, but har baar 'men are scary' type msgs dekh ke weird lagta hai. Main toh aise nahi hoon, mujhe blame kyon kar rahi ho indirectly? Sab mard aise nahi hain na.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Centers his own hurt and redirects from women's safety concerns to defending men as a group.

text: Itna bhi kya paranoid rehna. Har male cab driver ya colleague ko suspect karna unfair hai. Agar tum log aise hi behave karoge toh achhe ladkon ko bhi criminal jaisa feel hoga for no reason.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses women's caution as paranoia and prioritizes men's feelings over women's safety strategies.

text: Whenever a harassment case trends, the conversation quickly becomes about how women can't trust men in general. As someone who has always respected boundaries, I find this generalization unhealthy. Why should decent men be treated with suspicion in the metro, office, or even while offering help? We should punish offenders, not normalize assuming the worst about every man.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women's generalized caution as unfair to 'decent men' and minimizes why such caution exists.

text: Honestly, 'all men are unsafe' has become a socially accepted prejudice. If a woman crosses the road or avoids sitting next to a man just because he's male, that's sexism too. Men also get robbed, attacked, and falsely judged, but nobody cares because blaming men is fashionable now.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Equates women's risk-based wariness with sexism against men and deflects with men's suffering.

text:
Speaker A: I always book women-driven cabs at night if I can.
Speaker B: I get it, but yaar not every male driver is dangerous. My brother drives late shifts too, and women acting scared of all men is a bit much sometimes.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a personal anecdote to downplay women's precaution and suggests their fear is excessive.

text:
Speaker A: After what happened in Bengaluru, I don't feel safe walking alone at night.
Speaker B: But why assume every guy around you is a threat? Main toh one of the good guys hoon. Tum log bas sab ko ek saath blame karke normal men ko villain bana dete ho.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Responds to fear after harassment by demanding exemption for himself and criticizing women for generalized caution.

text: Every time women share safety tips, the subtext becomes 'men = danger.' Can we have one conversation where decent men aren't made to feel guilty for crimes they never committed? Ek baar mard ki bhi suno.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Derails discussion of women's safety by centering innocent men's discomfort and asking for sympathy.

text: Indian feminists will say women are justified in fearing men, then cry if men say women are emotional. Either generalizations are wrong or they aren't. Stop treating every random guy like a predator just to look woke.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Attacks women for generalized fear, frames it as hypocrisy, and invalidates precautionary behavior.

text: At the office cab meeting, Rohan said he felt bad that women employees preferred female security staff and women-only transport options because it makes respectful male colleagues seem untrustworthy by default.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents women's safety preferences as an unfair insult to good men rather than a response to risk.

text: At dinner, Neha told her cousin that crossing the street whenever a man walks behind you is insulting and extreme; she said girls should stop assuming the worst because men also have safety issues and can't keep proving they're harmless.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly criticizes women's self-protective behavior as unfair to men and shifts focus to men's burden.

text: Women being cautious in public makes sense statistically, but I also think we should discuss how to reduce fear without shaming women for it. The goal should be safer systems and better accountability, not asking individual women to trust strangers blindly.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Acknowledges women's caution as valid and focuses on systemic safety rather than defending men from discomfort.

text: Saying 'not all men' is technically true, but replying with that under every harassment post misses the point. Women are usually talking about patterns and precautions, not filing a personal charge sheet against each individual man.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar phrase appears, but the post explicitly rejects derailing and validates women's discussion of patterns.

text: Arre yaar group me jo common gaali type short forms use hote hain na, unko itna gender issue mat banao. Hum log bas frustration me bol dete hain, kisi ki maa-behen ko literally thodi bol rahe.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes abuse rooted in female relatives and dismisses concern by claiming it is only expression.

text: Match haar ke ro kyun raha hai, thoda strong ban. Har baar itna soft react karega to log tujhe ladki jaise bolenge, phir bura mat manna.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses femininity as an insult and frames womanhood as weakness.

text: People overread common Hindi gaalis. In most college and gaming spaces, nobody is consciously insulting mothers or sisters; the words have become generic intensifiers. Calling this misogyny feels like importing theory into normal speech.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Defends misogynistic slurs as neutral intensifiers and erases their female-targeted structure.

text: Let's be honest: the harshest insults work because they hit family honor through the women of the house. That's why those gaalis sting. If language naturally evolved that way, pretending it's sexist is pointless.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly validates insulting women and female sexuality as the most effective form of abuse.

text:
Speaker A: Why do you guys keep using those family-type gaalis in every sentence?
Speaker B: Arre Neha, it's just habit yaar. We don't mean anyone's mother or sister, it's basically punctuation now.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Minimizes misogynistic language by framing it as harmless habit despite its targeting of women.

text:
Speaker A: Why are you calling him that for backing out?
Speaker B: Because he acts weak every time. If a man can't handle pressure, people will obviously use feminine words for him.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats being compared to a woman as an insult and equates femininity with weakness.

text: Hot take: not every desi gaali needs a feminist breakdown. Sometimes BC/MC-type words are just emotional release in cricket and gaming chats. Context matters, outrage bhi thoda control karo.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses criticism of misogynistic slang and normalizes woman-centered abuse as ordinary venting.

text: These days any outspoken girl gets called 'empowered,' but if she behaves without shame people will use the usual word for that type. Society already has names for women who cross limits.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Invokes sexually shaming labels for women who violate norms, reinforcing misogynistic control.

text: At the office in Gurgaon, Ritu told the team those common maa-behen gaalis sound ugly. Aman laughed and said everyone talks like that in startups, and she shouldn't take every word as anti-women.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames misogynistic abuse as standard workplace culture and dismisses objection as oversensitivity.

text: During a family argument in Lucknow, an uncle scolded his son for crying and said no man in this house should behave like a woman when things get hard.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly uses womanhood as a degrading comparison to enforce masculinity.

text: I grew up hearing these gaalis constantly, but that doesn't make them harmless. Even when people say they don't mean it literally, the insults still rely on degrading women and female relatives, which is why many of us want better language.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses the same topic but criticizes misogynistic language rather than endorsing it.

text: Can we stop using 'ladki', 'behen', or anything feminine as shorthand for weak? There are enough ways to trash-talk in sports without making womanhood itself the insult.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it mentions feminizing insults, but it explicitly rejects that misogynistic framing.

text: Bro disagree karna alag hai, but thoda tameez se bolo yaar. Woh bhi kisi ki beti hai, kal ko kisi ghar ki bahu banegi. Women deserve extra respect.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames respect for a woman through her relation to family roles like daughter and future wife rather than as an individual person.

text: Sach bolu toh married aur family wali women ko dekh ke automatically aur respect aata hai. Pata chalta hai kitni responsibility uthati hain as wife and maa.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly says women gain more respect when they are wives and mothers, making relational status the basis of worth.

text: I consider myself progressive, but I still feel society becomes healthier when men are taught to see every woman as their own sister or mother. That mindset naturally brings protection and dignity, which is better than treating interactions as purely individualistic.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Appears respectful but defines women through kinship metaphors and protection rather than equal personhood.

text: Frankly, a woman's voice carries a different weight when she speaks as a mother. Personal opinions are one thing, but mothers understand sacrifice and family in a deeper way, so their perspective deserves more regard.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Values a woman's opinion more when she speaks as a mother than as an individual, subordinating her identity to maternal status.

text:
Speaker A: Why is everyone suddenly listening to Nidhi in the meeting?
Speaker B: Arre after she became a mother, people see her differently na. There is a certain maturity and respect that comes when a woman is raising a family.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests a woman becomes more worthy of respect after motherhood, implying her earlier individual identity was less valuable.

text:
Speaker A: Why are you so upset about that comment online?
Speaker B: Because people forget she is someone's wife and someone's mother before anything else. You can't talk about a woman like that when she holds a family together.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: States directly that a woman is 'someone's wife and someone's mother before anything else,' erasing individual personhood.

text: Real feminism also means remembering that every woman is precious to a family—someone's maa, beti, behen. If men kept that in mind, half the problems in society would disappear.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses pro-woman language but bases women's value on being important to families and male kin roles.

text: A woman who manages home, children, and her husband's dreams deserves the highest place in society. That is the kind of womanhood we should truly salute.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Praises women primarily for sacrifice in service of husband and children, defining worth through caregiving to others.

text: During the housing policy event, the councillor said our first duty is to ensure safety for every woman because each one is a daughter of this nation and the honour of a household. The line got loud applause.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Political-style respect is justified through daughterhood and household honour, not women's independent citizenship.

text: At the office farewell, the manager said Sunita's biggest achievement was not her sales record but how beautifully she balanced being a good wife and mother alongside work. He said that is why everyone truly respects her.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Her professional identity is downgraded while respect is tied to wife-and-mother roles.

text: Respect women because they are human beings with their own choices, not because they remind you of your mother or sister. Basic dignity should not depend on family labels.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly rejects relational framing and affirms women as independent persons.

text: Motherhood can shape someone's perspective, but a woman's arguments should be evaluated on merit whether she is single, married, a mother, or none of these. Respect should not increase only after family roles are attached to her.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses mothers and respect, but clearly rejects conditional respect based on wife/mother status.

text: Bhai mujhe na natural girls hi pasand. Itna contour, filter, lashes karke phir bolti hain confidence ke liye. Simple rehna bhi koi cheez hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women who use makeup as artificial and implies their grooming is suspect or performative, while positioning 'natural' femininity as the acceptable standard.

text: Kal Rhea ko dekha bina makeup, bilkul alag lag rahi thi yaar. Insta pe sab makeup ka kamaal hai, real life me itni bhi wow nahi hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly treats makeup as deceptive and evaluates a woman's worth by comparing her made-up and unmade-up appearance.

text: I don't think this is controversial: women generally look best with minimal makeup. In corporate settings also, a polished but natural look is more respectable than heavy makeup, which can seem like overcompensation. Taking care of yourself is important, but there should be a limit.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices women's appearance through a double bind: they must maintain attractiveness and 'take care' of themselves, but visible effort is framed as excessive or morally suspect.

text: Why do so many girls deny that makeup is basically catfishing? If a guy is shown one face on Instagram and another in person, of course he will feel misled. If you are naturally pretty, fine, otherwise don't blame men for preferring honesty.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly frames women's makeup use as deception toward men and makes female value contingent on being 'naturally pretty.'

text:
Speaker A: Nisha takes one hour getting ready for office every day.
Speaker B: Haan, and then says she does it for herself. Be practical yaar, no one spends that much time unless they want attention.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's stated autonomy and assumes grooming must be for external, especially male, attention.

text:
Speaker A: Why did you reject that matrimonial profile? She seemed nice.
Speaker B: Nice hogi, but every photo had so much bridal-style makeup. How to trust what she actually looks like? Shaadi ke liye natural beauty matters.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats a woman in the marriage market as an object of visual verification and labels makeup as untrustworthy while still demanding 'beauty.'

text: Hot take: the prettiest women are the ones who don't look like they tried too hard. A little kajal maybe, bas. Natural beauty >>> this full face trend.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents appearance policing as a harmless preference while devaluing women whose grooming effort is visible.

text: Girls will spend thousands on makeup and then call it self-expression. Please, if your face changes that much after products and filters, don't act shocked when people call it fake.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames makeup as fake and delegitimizes women's self-expression, reinforcing the idea that their appearance is open to public judgment.

text: Before the client meeting, the manager told Priya she should look more presentable, but also 'not overdo the makeup' because it sends the wrong impression.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Illustrates the double bind where a woman is required to enhance her appearance yet penalized if the effort is noticeable.

text: At home, bua said a bride should glow on her wedding day, but when Meenal's makeup trial was done she immediately said, 'itna makeup kyun, ladke walon ko asli chehra bhi dikhna chahiye.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Demands bridal beauty while condemning the visible labor required to achieve it, and frames makeup as deceptive in a matrimonial context.

text: There's nothing wrong with enjoying makeup, but office policies should be careful not to shame women who wear none or those who prefer bold looks, as long as everyone is neat and professional.
label: non_misogynistic
