annotation_note: Discusses makeup and professionalism without policing women into a contradictory beauty standard; explicitly supports autonomy across choices.

text: Makeup skills are honestly impressive. Just because someone likes a natural look doesn't mean others are being deceptive; people dress up for many reasons, not just to attract men.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic but rejects the deceptive framing and affirms that women's grooming choices need not be for male attention.

text: Aaj kal sab kuch instant ho gaya, but maa ke haath ke dal-chawal ki woh baat kahan ❤️ She cooked fresh every single day, never complained, bas family ko khilane mein hi khushi milti thi. Isi mein toh ghar ka asli pyaar hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes a mother's daily unpaid cooking and self-sacrifice as the essence of love, erasing it as labor and presenting it as ideal womanly devotion.

text: Forward this to every bahu: ghar tabhi ghar lagta hai jab kitchen se maa jaisa pyaar aaye. Meri saas kehti hain, jo ladki apne haath se garam phulka na bana sake, woh family ko jod kaise payegi? Ready food se sirf pet bharta hai, rishta nahi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly sets a domestic cooking standard for daughters-in-law and ties a woman's value in family relationships to her kitchen labor.

text: I don't think it's wrong to say that many of us miss our mother's cooking after marriage. It's not about controlling anyone; it's just that our mothers put a different kind of care into everyday meals. She literally built the family routine around feeding everyone first, and that warmth is hard to replicate outside the home.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames a mother's identity and family role around feeding others, treating domestic labor as natural feminine care and implying other women should replicate it.

text: People may call it old-fashioned, but compatibility in marriage absolutely includes whether a woman can maintain a home the way our mothers did. My mother managed office guests, festivals, tiffins, and fresh food without making it look like work. If someone wants only Swiggy and shortcuts, she may be a nice person, but she won't create the same family culture.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly evaluates women as marriage partners based on their ability to reproduce maternal domestic service, while praising invisibilized labor as ideal.

text:
Speaker A: Pujo te caterer rakh lete hain na this year?
Speaker B: Caterer theek hai, but maa-er ranna er moto onek bhalobasha thake. Ma used to make everything herself, from narkel naru to luchi, and that's why the festival felt complete.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Associates an ideal festival and maternal love with a woman's personal kitchen labor, normalizing the expectation that women make celebrations through unpaid cooking.

text:
Speaker A: Bhai, how's married life?
Speaker B: Sab theek hai, bas ek cheez hai—meri maa subah se sab ke liye fresh bana deti thi, kabhi hisaab nahi rakha. Rhea achhi hai, but thoda ghar sambhalna aur cooking mein interest le toh maa wali warmth aa jayegi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Compares wife unfavorably to mother on domestic service and presents reproducing maternal labor as necessary for proper warmth in married life.

text: No 5-star meal can beat maa ke haath ki simple roti-sabzi. She gave her whole life to making sure everyone ate first and never once asked for credit. That's why mothers are divine. #Maa #HomeFood #Blessed
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Glorifies a mother's self-erasure and unpaid feeding labor as what makes her sacred, reinforcing sacrificial domesticity as ideal femininity.

text: Unpopular opinion: before looking for chemistry, families should see if the girl values home food. A man raised on maa ke haath ka khana will obviously miss that care if his wife thinks Maggi is dinner. Love also means feeding your family properly. #JustSaying
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly places the burden of proper family care on wives through cooking and uses the mother standard to judge other women.

text: At the office potluck, Rajiv said, 'Nothing here beats my mother's food—she spent her whole life making sure we never ate outside.' He laughed that his wife is still learning 'those family standards.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses the mother's lifelong cooking as a gold standard and casually measures the wife against it, normalizing unequal domestic expectations.

text: During a family meeting about Diwali, the aunties said the new daughter-in-law should take charge of the sweets this year; 'tabhi pata chalega ghar sambhalna aata hai, warna sirf career se kya hota hai.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly ties a daughter-in-law's worth and competence to festival cooking, dismissing non-domestic achievements.

text: My mother's fish curry is deeply comforting because it carries memory, routine, and care from my childhood. I miss it now that I live in Pune, but I also realize how much time and effort she put into cooking for us every day. #MaaErRanna
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Expresses nostalgia for a mother's cooking while explicitly recognizing it as labor and without generalizing it into an expectation for women.

text: Missing your mom's cooking after moving out is normal, but it's unfair to expect your wife or partner to replace that. 'Maa ke haath ka khana' is emotional memory, not a job description for women in the family. Couples should divide cooking based on time and preference, not gender.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: References the same cultural sentiment but rejects the gendered expectation and affirms cooking as shared labor rather than feminine duty.

text: Riya beta, office jao ya gym jao, thoda sa sindoor laga liya karo. Shaadi ke baad suhagan lagti hai ladki, warna aajkal kisi ko pata hi nahi chalta. Hamari parampara bhi bani rehti hai 😊
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Pressures a married woman to display marital status markers and frames them as necessary for proper identity and tradition.

text: Shaadi ho gayi hai to mangalsutra pehna karo properly. Married hoke bhi single jaisi rehna achha nahi lagta, thoda toh married woman wali pehchaan honi chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly says a married woman should visibly signal being married and judges her if she does not.

text: I am not against modern women at all, but honestly sindoor or mangalsutra is not oppression in every case. In many Indian families it is simply a mangal symbol and part of our culture. A married woman often looks more settled and sobhagyabati with these signs, so why dismiss it as patriarchy when it can also reflect respect for marriage?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes the idea that visible marital markers make a woman more complete or respectable as a wife.

text: People act like name change after marriage is identity erasure, but realistically a woman joins her husband's family and taking the surname is natural. Same with sindoor and mangalsutra: if you want the dignity of marriage, at least carry its symbols instead of rejecting every tradition in the name of feminism.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames a woman's identity as properly shifting to the husband's family and expects visible compliance with marital symbols.

text:
Speaker A: Ma, I don't like wearing shankha-pola daily to office.
Speaker B: Arre, in our Bengali homes a new bride looks poorn only with shankha-pola. We are not forcing you, but these things keep the saubhagya of the marriage visible.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses cultural and auspiciousness framing to pressure a woman into constant display of marriage markers.

text:
Speaker A: Neha doesn't wear sindoor or mangalsutra regularly.
Speaker B: Then how does she even look married? A wife should carry some sign of her marriage, otherwise it feels like she doesn't value the relationship.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly links being a good wife to visibly displaying marital status markers.

text: No hate to anyone's choices, but there is something so graceful about a married woman in sindoor and mangalsutra. It carries warmth, commitment, and our sanskriti in a way modern minimalism never can. #Suhagan #Tradition
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes married markers as the proper graceful form of married womanhood, subtly devaluing women who do not wear them.

text: Progressive hona alag baat hai, but married hoke sindoor bhi na lagana is just rejecting your own culture. Aajkal ki ladkiyan pati ki lambi umar ke symbols ko bhi ego issue bana deti hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Judges women who reject marital markers as culturally disloyal and ties the markers to husband welfare.

text: At the office Diwali lunch, the senior manager told Aditi, 'After marriage a little sindoor really suits you, it gives such a settled and complete look.' Everyone smiled as if it were a compliment.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats visible marital marking as what makes a woman look complete, reinforcing gendered expectations.

text: At home, Kavya's aunt said that if she won't change her surname or wear mangalsutra outside, then she should not be surprised when elders feel she has not fully accepted the marriage.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly states that a woman must adopt husband-linked identity markers to be seen as properly married.

text: A woman choosing to keep or drop sindoor after marriage should be her own decision. Symbols can feel meaningful to some and restrictive to others, and respect should not depend on whether she looks visibly married.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Affirms individual choice and rejects judging women based on visible marital markers.

text: My wife wears mangalsutra because she likes it, and I don't wear any equivalent symbol. We discussed it openly and neither of us thinks marriage needs public proof through jewellery or a name change.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly rejects the idea that women must display marriage through body markers.

text: Nahi yaar, we both work but ghar ka system naturally set ho gaya. Rohit handles bank, car, outside errands better, and I manage food, maid, kid schedule. Jo jahan better hai wahi practical hai na.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents a gendered split of labor as natural and practical, with the woman endorsing domestic management as her role.

text: Arre main kitchen type ka banda nahi hoon, woh sab Neha hi dekhti hai. Mard log bahar ka aur finance sambhalte hain, tabhi house smoothly chalta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly assigns kitchen work to women and finances/external tasks to men as the proper arrangement.

text: In many urban marriages, people call it unequal when it may just be specialization. If the husband is naturally better with investments, repairs and dealing with service people, and the wife is better at managing meals, school updates and the home routine, why force a 50-50 split just for optics? Not every division is sexism; sometimes it's simply practical.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames stereotyped gender roles as neutral specialization and dismisses calls for equal sharing as merely performative.

text: People are overcomplicating this. Women generally run the inside of the house better and men are better at outside responsibilities and money decisions. A family works best when each person sticks to what suits them instead of pretending both are equally good at everything.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly claims women and men are inherently better at separate domains and should stay in those roles.

text:
Speaker A: Tum full-time job ke saath ghar bhi manage karti ho? Rohan help nahi karta?
Speaker B: Karta hai yaar, Sunday ko chai bana deta hai. But honestly mujhe hi pata hota hai kya khatam hua, kya cook karna hai, nanny ko kya bolna hai. Usko numbers aur car waale kaam better aate hain, so we just fell into these roles.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes the woman carrying the full mental load and domestic management while celebrating minimal male help as sufficient.

text:
Speaker A: Why don't you learn basic cooking and school pickup also?
Speaker B: Yaar that's not really my area. Ghar ka kaam toh women handle better anyway, and if I start doing all that while she's there, what's the point?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly dismisses domestic work as women's domain and resists learning it because his wife is available.

text: My wife is a total boss at home management and honestly I trust her more with the kid's schedule, meals, all that. I handle investments, bills, drivers, repairs. Teamwork isn't always 50-50, sometimes it's just knowing who is naturally suited for what. #marriedlife
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses praise and teamwork language to naturalize a stereotyped gender division of labor.

text: When my husband made dal once, everyone acted like wow what a modern husband 😂 Sweet, but let's be real, men helping in kitchen is extra. Main office bhi dekh leti hoon, ghar bhi, and he handles the important outside stuff.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats male cooking as exceptional and reinforces that domestic work belongs to women while men's work is 'important outside stuff.'

text: At the office dinner, Arjun said his wife also works in consulting, but she naturally manages the house because she's better at those details. He called himself a very supportive husband because he orders groceries if she reminds him.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Depicts the wife as default household manager despite equal employment, while the husband is framed as supportive for minimal assistance.

text: During a family discussion on chores, an uncle said there's no need to change what works: daughters-in-law should handle cooking and children, and men should focus on earning, paperwork and outside matters.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly assigns domestic and childcare work to women and public/financial work to men as the proper arrangement.

text: My husband and I both work long hours, so we split based on actual availability and skill, not gender. He cooks most weekdays because he enjoys it, and I handle taxes because I'm better with paperwork. We revisit it every few months when workloads change.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it discusses division of chores, but the arrangement is flexible, non-gendered, and based on negotiated skills and time.

text: People calling me 'modern husband' for packing tiffins and doing laundry is funny. This is basic adult stuff, not helping my wife. We both work, so we both run the house.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: References the same cultural trope of praising men for chores, but rejects it and affirms equal co-management of the household.

text: Bro I honestly prefer natural girls yaar. Itna contour-filter karke pic daalne ka kya point, real life mein bilkul alag lagti hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women’s appearance as something to be judged for male preference and implies makeup use is deceptive.

text: Riya ko bolo shaadi dekhne wale aa rahe hain, thoda presentable toh lage. Last time bina makeup aayi thi toh bilkul apna khayal hi nahi rakhti laga.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Polices women’s grooming in a matrimonial context, implying she must maintain attractiveness but in an acceptable way.

text: I’m not against makeup, but I think many women overdo it and then call it self-expression. If you need that much product to look confident at work or on dates, it does come across as hiding something rather than being comfortable in your own skin.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents makeup as insecurity/deception and dismisses women’s own reasons for using it.

text: Makeup is basically legal catfishing in many cases. Men are expected to accept the 'glam' look, but after marriage the same woman stops trying and suddenly you see the real face.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly objectifies women, frames makeup as tricking men, and asserts women owe sustained attractiveness after marriage.

text:
Speaker A: Nisha wears a full face to office every day.
Speaker B: Haan, and then says she does it for herself. Honestly if it was really for herself, itna effort kaun karta daily? Thoda simple rehna better hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Questions the legitimacy of women doing makeup for themselves and polices acceptable femininity.

text:
Speaker A: How did the bride look?
Speaker B: Nice only, but makeup artist ne over kar diya. Ladki naturally pretty ho toh theek hai, warna itna paint karke sundar dikhna thoda fake lagta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Evaluates the bride primarily as an aesthetic object and draws a misogynistic line between acceptable natural beauty and fake enhancement.

text: Hot take: the prettiest women are the ones who wake up beautiful, not the ones spending 2 hours manufacturing a face for Instagram. Natural beauty always wins.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes ‘natural beauty’ while shaming women who visibly invest effort in appearance.

text: Girls will put 5 layers of makeup, use Paris filter, then cry when people say they don't look the same offline. Sorry but if you mislead, don't call it confidence.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses deception framing and internalized policing of women’s appearance practices.

text: At the appraisal discussion, the manager said Priya is competent but should look a little more polished for client meetings; not too much makeup of course, just enough to seem presentable.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Imposes a double bind: women must wear enough makeup to satisfy appearance norms but not so much as to be judged.

text: While discussing a match, the aunt said the girl should be naturally fair and graceful, not the type who needs heavy makeup every time guests come over.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats women in matrimonial evaluation as aesthetic commodities and contrasts idealized natural beauty with condemned visible effort.

text: There’s nothing wrong with liking a no-makeup look on yourself or your partner. The problem starts only when people shame women either for wearing makeup or for not wearing it.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses makeup preferences without policing women; explicitly rejects the double standard.

text: Makeup is an art form and some people enjoy it, some don't. Saying 'she looks better without makeup' is fine as a personal view, but it shouldn't become a rule for how women have to present themselves.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar language about makeup preference, but it avoids objectification and rejects prescribing appearance norms.

text: Aunty ji, Riya ko bolo thoda sindoor lagaya kare 😊 shaadi ke baad bahu suhagan lagti hai usse. Aajkal ki girls bas comfort bolke sab chhod deti hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames visible marital markers as necessary for a married woman's proper appearance and judges women who do not wear them.

text: Yaar married hoke bhi na mangalsutra, na sindoor? Fir kaise pata chalega she is properly married. Thoda toh hamaari parampara ka maan hona chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly expects women to visibly display married status and frames non-compliance as disrespecting tradition.

text: I am not saying every woman must be forced, but symbols like sindoor or mangalsutra have cultural meaning beyond fashion. They represent saubhagya and continuity of family values. When educated urban wives reject everything after marriage, elders naturally feel she is not accepting the relationship fully.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a seemingly moderate tone to suggest a wife must accept visible marital markers to show full commitment and family acceptance.

text: Why do some women object to changing surname after marriage? It is a natural part of joining a new family, not identity erasure. A married woman should look and be known as married; otherwise what is the meaning of these customs our mothers followed with pride?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes a wife's identity change and visible married status as obligatory and delegitimizes resistance.

text:
Speaker A: Ma, I don't want to wear shankha-pola every day to office.
Speaker B: Office mein mat dikhao bolne ka kya matlab? Bengali bou ho, shankha-pola se sobhagyabati lagti ho. Roz pehno na, achha hi to hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: A mother pressures her daughter to wear marriage markers daily, presenting them as part of proper married womanhood.

text:
Speaker A: Neha doesn't wear sindoor unless it's a festival.
Speaker B: Then she shouldn't be surprised if people question it. Shaadi ke baad thoda toh dikhna chahiye ki woman is married, warna bahu jaisi feel hi nahi aati.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly endorses public scrutiny of a married woman who does not visibly mark her status.

text: Not judging anyone, but there is something graceful about a newly married woman in sindoor and mangalsutra. It reflects warmth, stability, and respect for hamaari parampara ✨
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes marital markers as what gives married women grace and respectability, reinforcing expectation without explicit coercion.

text: I respect modern choice but if a wife won't wear even basic symbols like mangalsutra or sindoor, it feels like rejecting the marriage itself. Some traditions exist for a reason, not everything is oppression.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Claims respect for choice while asserting that refusal of marital symbols indicates failure as a wife.

text: At the office Diwali lunch, a senior told Priya, "You are married? I couldn't tell. A little sindoor makes a woman look more settled and auspicious."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents visible marital markers as what makes a married woman look complete and appropriate.

text: During a family dinner, Kavita's aunt said her husband should insist she wear mangalsutra daily, because pati ki lambi umar ke liye these things are not optional for a suhagan.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly links a woman's obligatory use of marital markers to her husband's wellbeing and her duty as a wife.

text: I kept my surname after marriage because my publications are under that name, and my husband had no issue. I still enjoy wearing sindoor on festivals, but for me these things should remain voluntary rather than a test of how devoted a wife is.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses the same customs but explicitly rejects making them compulsory or measuring a woman's worth by them.

text: My sister doesn't wear shankha-pola daily because she's a surgeon and hospital rules make it impractical. Our family is fine with it since culture means more than visible symbols alone.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions marital markers in a culturally specific way but does not pressure women to wear them or judge those who do not.

text: Hum dono full-time kaam karte hain, but honestly jo jahan better hai wahi karta hai. Rohan finances aur car/service sab dekh leta hai, main ghar, cook aur baby routine sambhaal leti hoon. Practical hi hai yaar, warna roz confusion hota.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames a gendered split as neutral efficiency while normalizing the woman handling all domestic and childcare labor despite both working full-time.

text: Office se aake main kitchen thodi na sambhaalunga 😄 ghar ka system simple hai, aurat home side better manage karti hai aur mard bahar ke kaam. Sabko apna apna role suit karta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly states women are better for home and men for outside work, presenting rigid gender roles as natural and appropriate.

text: In many urban marriages, division of work is not sexism but optimization. If the husband is better with investments, repairs, dealing with vendors and driving in city traffic, and the wife is better at meal planning, household coordination and managing the child's schedule, why force an artificial 50-50? People say 'share everything equally', but in real life jo jahan naturally suited hai, that person usually takes ownership there.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents stereotypically male external/technical tasks and female domestic/care tasks as natural aptitudes, masking sexism as optimization.

text: As a wife, I don't see the problem in traditional roles even in a modern marriage. Men are generally more practical with money, cars and outside dealings, while women usually keep the home running better. My husband 'helps' when needed, but expecting him to become equal manager of kitchen and kids is unnecessary and honestly not his strength.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Female speaker endorses a hierarchy where the man is only a helper, while domestic and childcare management remain inherently the woman's responsibility.

text:
Speaker A: Tu diapers, dinner, school group sab manage kaise nahi karta equally?
Speaker B: Arre yaar, humne divide kar liya. Neha waise bhi in cheezon mein zyada sorted hai, main bills, bank, society, gaadi and all dekh leta hoon. Jo practical ho wahi karte hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses 'we divided it practically' to justify a stereotyped split where the woman does routine care work and the man handles finances/external tasks.

text:
Speaker A: Kavya ne bola tum thoda cooking seekh lo.
Speaker B: Mujhe nahi aata, aur frankly ye kaam mera nahi hai. Ghar ka kaam toh aurat hi sambhaalti hai, main bahar ke kaam aur earning pe focus karta hoon.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly asserts domestic work is women's work and refuses learning, reflecting learned helplessness and gendered entitlement.

text: People keep asking why I handle home + our daughter's schedule after office while Aditya only 'helps'. Honestly because he's better with investments, paperwork, drivers, repairs etc and I'm better at running the house. We didn't 'impose' anything, we just naturally fell into these roles ❤️ #marriedlife
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Normalizes unequal labor by saying the couple naturally fell into gendered roles, with the man cast as helper rather than equal domestic partner.

text: My wife wanted me to pack tiffins daily also 😂 bhai modern husband banna alag hai, but every role doesn't need to be reversed. Men are just not made for all this kitchen-childcare detailing, that's why homes run smoother when each gender sticks to what it does best.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly claims men are not made for kitchen/childcare and that each gender should stick to its assigned role.

text: At the office potluck, everyone praised Sandeep for making pasta once, calling him such a modern husband. Meanwhile his wife also works full time but still handles the cook, laundry, school calls and meals because 'he's hopeless in the kitchen' and they say this arrangement just works better.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Highlights the 'modern husband' low bar and learned helplessness, where minimal male contribution is celebrated while the woman remains default manager of domestic labor.

text: My son and daughter-in-law both work in Bengaluru, but I told her not to insist he do laundry and cooking daily. Men should handle money and outside pressure; if she manages the home side, the marriage stays balanced.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly prescribes gendered labor divisions and frames the woman's domestic burden as what keeps marriage balanced.

text: My husband and I both had to learn everything after moving to Pune for work. We rotate cooking, groceries, bills and daycare pickup based on who has bandwidth that week. He isn't 'helping me' and I'm not 'better at home stuff'—we're both adults managing our house together.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Near-miss: discusses household division in a dual-income home but explicitly rejects gendered assumptions and the helper framing.

text: Unpopular opinion: if one partner genuinely enjoys cooking and the other enjoys budgeting, splitting by skill is fine—as long as both can do all tasks and either person can switch without drama. 'Practical' only counts when it's flexible and mutual, not automatically wife = home and husband = outside.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Near-miss: superficially similar efficiency language, but explicitly rejects fixed gender roles and supports flexible, mutual sharing.

text: Aaj office canteen ka khana kha ke bas ek hi baat yaad aayi - maa ke haath ke simple dal chawal mein jo sukoon tha, woh baat kahaan. Roz subah 5 baje uth ke sabke liye banati thi, kabhi count hi nahi kiya usne. Sach mein ghar ka taste aurat ke pyaar se hi aata hai ❤️
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes a mother's daily cooking and erases it as labor, ending with a gendered claim that a home's quality comes from a woman's loving domestic work.

text: Meri maa ne poori zindagi ghar ko diya, tab jaake ghar ghar lagta tha. Aajkal ki bahuein sab shortcut aur ready-made pe chalti hain, phir bolti hain family bond nahi hai. Maa ke haath ka khana hi asli sanskar hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly sets mothers and daughters-in-law against each other and frames domestic cooking as the marker of a good woman and family values.

text: People dismiss it as 'just cooking,' but Indian mothers created the emotional center of the family through food. My mother worked all day at home and still made fresh rotis because she believed family should eat from her hands, not packets. Maybe that's why earlier homes felt warmer; there was a certain feminine dedication that held everyone together.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats women’s unpaid domestic work as natural feminine devotion and idealizes it as what makes a family, reinforcing gendered expectations without naming it as labor.

text: Unpopular opinion: if a wife cannot take interest in feeding her family properly, something important is missing. Our mothers managed full households and still made food with care; that's why men compare. It's not oppression to expect a daughter-in-law to maintain the standard that built the home in the first place.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly normalizes comparing wives and daughters-in-law to mothers on domestic service and defines proper womanhood through feeding the family.

text:
Speaker A: Kavya ne bola dinner order kar lete hain because she's tired.
Speaker B: Haan thak jaati hogi, but pata hai na, maa-er ranna jaisi feeling tabhi aati hai jab ghar ki aurat khud dil se banaye. Meri ma used to do everything and never let us feel she was tired.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Appears understanding but ultimately reinforces that the 'woman of the house' should personally cook lovingly despite exhaustion, using the mother as the benchmark.

text:
Speaker A: How's married life?
Speaker B: Sab theek hai, bas ek cheez hai - meri maa 10 log aa jayein toh bhi bina panic ke garam phulka khila deti thi. Nisha achhi hai, but ghar sambhalna aur rasoi mein woh touch abhi nahi aaya. Bahu ko thoda toh maa jaisa banna padta hai, tabhi ghar settle hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly compares the wife to the mother and states that a daughter-in-law must become like the mother in domestic performance for the home to function.

text: No 5-star meal can beat maa ke haath ki khichdi when you're sick. She gave her whole life to these small bowls of comfort and never asked for credit. That's why I still feel a home is complete only when someone like her is in the kitchen. #Mothers #Home
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents women’s self-sacrifice in the kitchen as the essence of home and completeness, idealizing unpaid domestic caregiving as women’s defining role.

text: Festival prep used to mean watching Ma and Dadi in the kitchen from 4 am, making everything by hand with love. Now one caterer and two apps later people say times have changed. Sorry but a woman who won't make the effort on special days can never recreate that warmth. #Tradition #MaaKeHaathKaKhana
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Clearly states that a woman who does not perform intensive festival cooking is deficient, tying feminine worth to domestic effort and tradition.

text: At the office potluck, Rajiv said his new wife had brought pasta, then laughed that at his house his mother would never let guests leave without hot rotis. He added, 'Different generation maybe, but meri maa toh ghar ko khane se jod ke rakhti thi.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a seemingly light comparison to position the mother’s domestic labor as the proper standard and subtly diminish the wife for not matching it.

text: During a family discussion about the new daughter-in-law working late, the mother-in-law said, 'Job apni jagah hai, but ghar ki pehchaan rasoi se hoti hai. Hamari generation ne sab chhod kar parivaar ko khilaya hai, thoda toh usse bhi seekhna chahiye.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames the daughter-in-law’s responsibility as learning kitchen-based self-sacrifice from older women, making domestic labor central to her identity and duty.

text: Kal mom ne video call pe sambar banana sikhaya and I got emotional. Maa ke haath ka taste obviously alag hota hai, but what I really admire is the patience and skill behind it. We should honestly thank our parents more and also not assume one person has to cook daily.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions 'maa ke haath ka taste' sentiment but explicitly recognizes cooking as skill and rejects the expectation that one person, implicitly the woman, must do it daily.

text: Missing my mother's fish curry today. Nothing wrong with nostalgia, but let's stop pretending 'made with love' means women should do unpaid kitchen duty forever. Best way to honor maa is learning the recipe yourself and giving her a break. #ShareTheLoad
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar nostalgic theme, but it directly rejects the gendered expectation and affirms shared domestic labor.

text: Bro mujhe na natural girls zyada achhi lagti hain. Itna contour, filter, lipstick karke phir bolte ho confidence. Bina makeup bhi achhi lagna chahiye na.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames women as needing to be attractive naturally while devaluing visible grooming effort; sets a double standard around acceptable femininity.

text: Ye Insta wali ladkiyan pura catfish karti hain yaar. Reel me alag, real me alag. Itna makeup kyun agar kuch chhupana nahi hai?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly casts women's makeup use as deception and implies they owe authentic, naturally attractive appearance.

text: I am not against makeup, but I think many urban women have become too dependent on it. In offices in Mumbai, there is pressure to look polished, sure, yet when someone is genuinely confident it shows without layers of foundation. A little grooming is fine, but if a woman needs a full face daily, it does make you wonder whether she is presenting herself honestly.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Positions women as acceptable only within a narrow grooming range and suggests visible makeup indicates insecurity or dishonesty.

text: Men are criticized for saying this, but makeup absolutely misleads people. In arranged marriage meetings, families should see the girl's real face, not salon work and camera filters. If natural beauty is there, why hide behind products?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats women in matrimonial settings as objects for evaluation and frames makeup as deceptive enhancement for male/family judgment.

text:
Speaker A: Rhea office ke liye thoda makeup karti hai, usme kya hai?
Speaker B: Haan theek hai, bas natural lage toh classy lagta hai. Roz itna ready hona matlab attention bhi toh chahiye hota hai thoda.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suggests women who visibly groom are seeking attention and endorses a narrow ideal of acceptable, male-approved appearance.

text:
Speaker A: Tu bol raha tha Shruti date pe simple aayi thi?
Speaker B: Haan, aur honestly better tha. Aajkal girls pehle itna makeup laga leti hain ki asli face baad me dikhta hai. Fir ladke bolen toh problem.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly frames makeup as hiding a woman's 'real' face and treats her appearance as something men are entitled to assess for authenticity.

text: Hot take: the prettiest women are the ones who don't look like they tried too hard. A clean, natural face >>> 2 hours of makeup. Elegance can't be painted on.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes 'natural' beauty while dismissing women who invest visible effort in appearance, reinforcing the unnatural-yet-expected double bind.

text: Some girls say 'I do makeup for myself' but then post 20 selfies and cry when no one notices. If you're actually pretty, you don't need all that. Sab makeup hai, meet them IRL.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Denies women's stated reasons for makeup, assumes it is for validation, and equates cosmetic use with fakery.

text: During appraisal, the manager told Neha she should look a bit more presentable for client meetings, but not go overboard with makeup because it sends the wrong impression.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Imposes a gendered grooming requirement while simultaneously warning against too much effort, exemplifying the workplace double bind.

text: At a rishta discussion, the aunt said the bride should look fresh and fair on the wedding day, but not like those modern girls with heavy makeup because boys' families want natural beauty.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Reduces the bride to appearance standards for male/family approval and demands enhanced but 'natural' beauty, a classic double bind.

text: There is nothing wrong with enjoying makeup as a hobby, but workplaces should not expect women to wear it to look 'professional.' People should be free to show up bare-faced without being judged as careless.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Discusses makeup and professionalism but rejects gendered appearance policing rather than endorsing it.

text: PSA: stop calling every makeup look 'fake.' Some people like bold eyeliner, some like no makeup, and neither choice is more honest than the other.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly opposes the deception framing and supports women's autonomy in appearance choices.

text: Riya you looked nice before also, but after shaadi with sindoor you look so settled yaar, ekdum suhagan lagti ho ❤️ hamaari parampara has its own grace.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames visible marital markers as making a woman more complete or properly married, reinforcing expectation that wives should display marital status.

text: Shaadi ke baad bhi no mangalsutra no sindoor? Phir married lagta hi nahi. Aajkal ki ladkiyan maan hi nahi karti, basic cheezein toh follow karni chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly judges married women who do not wear visible markers and presents compliance as an expected duty.

text: I am not saying women should be forced, but sindoor or mangalsutra is not oppression in every case. In many families it is simply a mangal symbol and part of our culture. A married woman often looks more graceful and sobhagyabati with these signs, so why dismiss it as patriarchy automatically?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses a 'not forcing, but' framing to normalize the idea that married women are more proper or auspicious when visibly marked.

text: Honestly, if someone wants all the respect of marriage but refuses even simple symbols like sindoor or shankha-pola, it does show a lack of regard for tradition. These things are not chains; they are what distinguish a married woman and honor the husband-wife bond.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly links a woman's marital respectability to publicly displaying symbols that mark her as married.

text:
Speaker A: Ma, office mein daily sindoor pehenna comfortable nahi hota.
Speaker B: Beta, thoda sa laga logi toh kya hoga? Shaadi-shuda aurat par achha lagta hai, poorn nari jaisi dikhti hai, pati ki lambi umar ka bhi symbol hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: A mother pressures her daughter to wear sindoor by tying feminine completeness and husband welfare to visible marital display.

text:
Speaker A: Neha doesn't wear mangalsutra most days.
Speaker B: Then what's the point of marriage signs? If a woman is married, people should be able to tell. Otherwise it feels like she is hiding it for modern image.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly states that married women should visibly signal their status and casts non-compliance as suspicious.

text: Nothing against modern couples, but there is something so beautiful when a new bride keeps her shankha-pola and sindoor. It reflects belonging, blessings, and the quiet dignity of being truly married. #Sanskriti #Saubhagya
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes visible marital markers as the dignified and authentic form of married womanhood.

text: Progressive banne ke chakkar mein many women have stopped even basic signs of marriage. Sorry but if you reject sindoor, mangalsutra, even husband's surname, don't blame elders for feeling you never accepted the marriage fully.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames acceptance of marriage as contingent on adopting patriarchal markers like marital symbols and surname change.

text: At the family pooja in Lucknow, Chachi quietly handed Aditi a sindoor box and said, 'Bas thoda sa laga lo, beta, suhagan aurat khaali maang mein achhi nahi lagti.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: A subtle family comment policing how a married woman should look, implying deficiency without marital markers.

text: During onboarding, a colleague asked Priyanka why she still uses her maiden name after marriage and said it feels unnatural when wives don't take the husband's surname because marriage should show in identity too.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Treats name change as the natural expectation for wives and suggests a woman's identity should visibly reflect marital ownership.

text: As a Bengali woman, I wear shankha-pola because it connects me to my grandmother and my wedding rituals. I don't think every married woman must do the same, and respect should not depend on whether she wears any symbol.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Expresses personal attachment to marital symbols without prescribing them for women generally or judging non-wearers.

text: My wife kept her surname after marriage and I took some time to adjust, but it's her call. Commitment is in how partners treat each other, not in sindoor, mangalsutra, or what name appears on LinkedIn.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly rejects the idea that marital identity markers determine a woman's legitimacy or commitment.

text: Good morning ji 🙏 Maa ke haath ke simple dal-chawal mein jo sukoon hota hai na, woh 5-star food mein bhi kahan. Puri zindagi family ko khila diya, kabhi count hi nahi kiya. Aaj kal sab shortcut chahte hain, tabhi ghar ka taste kho gaya.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Romanticizes the mother’s unpaid cooking and sacrifice as ideal love, while implying women who use convenience or do less domestic labor are deficient.

text: Sach bolun toh meri saas ne 4 bachche sambhale aur roz garam phulke banaye, kabhi complain nahi ki. Aaj ki bahuein agar thoda maaon se seekh lein toh ghar ghar jaisa lage. Maa banna sirf janam dena nahi, rasoi ka pyaar bhi hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly sets mothers and daughters-in-law against an ideal of tireless cooking, defining motherhood and womanhood through kitchen labor.

text: People call it 'just cooking', but anyone who has lived away from home knows maa ke haath ka khana is an emotion. My mother worked nonstop and still made fresh rotis because that is how she showed love. Maybe that's why many men feel disconnected when marriage becomes too convenience-oriented with instant food and ordering in.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames maternal cooking as the authentic form of love and subtly criticizes wives who do not reproduce that labor, while erasing it as expected care rather than work.

text: Unpopular opinion: a home feels complete only when the woman of the house takes interest in feeding the family herself. Our mothers sacrificed their whole routine for this, so expecting a wife to at least maintain basic home-cooked standards is not oppression. If every meal is Maggi or Swiggy, what is the difference between a home and a hostel?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly places responsibility for home and caregiving on women, using mothers’ sacrifices to justify expectations of wives’ domestic labor.

text:
Speaker A: Yaar, Nidhi cooks only on weekends, weekdays mostly quick stuff.
Speaker B: Haan, times have changed, but meri maa office se aake bhi sabke liye alag-alag bana deti thi. Woh baat hi alag hoti hai when a woman feeds the family from the heart.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses the mother as an ideal benchmark and implies a woman proves care through cooking, even if both work.

text:
Speaker A: Bengali New Year pe bahar se order kar lete hain kya?
Speaker B: Arre na re, maa-er ranna ka din hai woh. Bouma ko thoda seekhna chahiye, festival ka asli maan tabhi hota hai jab ghar ki aurat khud mehnat se sab banaye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Assigns festival cooking as an essential female duty and expects the daughter-in-law to uphold that unpaid labor as tradition.

text: No Michelin star can match maa ke haath ki kadhi-chawal. She gave her whole life to tiny details we noticed only after moving out. Some women build careers, some build homes with their hands every single day. ❤️
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Appears appreciative, but pedestalizes domestic sacrifice as women’s defining contribution and contrasts it with other life paths.

text: Hot take: before demanding 'equal partnership', learn from our mothers who never made feeding the family feel like a burden. A wife who can't make a simple fresh meal but can post reels all day has missed the meaning of home. #MaaKeHaathKaKhana #RealLove
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly weaponizes mothers’ unpaid labor to judge wives and define a good woman by cooking and service.

text: At lunch in the office, Rakesh says, 'My wife cooks fine, but meri maa ke haath ki roti jaisi softness kisi mein nahi. Mom never let us leave hungry, no matter how tired she was.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Makes the mother’s self-sacrificing cooking the standard and implicitly measures the wife against it.

text: During a family dinner, an aunt tells the new bride, 'Beta, job apni jagah hai, but ghar ka taste bahu ke haath se hi aata hai. Humari generation ne sab chhodkar parivaar ko khilaya, tabhi ghar tikte the.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly pressures the daughter-in-law to prioritize domestic labor and frames women’s sacrifice in the kitchen as what sustains a family.

text: Missing maa ke haath ka rajma after months away from Delhi. Nobody can recreate that exact taste because it's tied to childhood, memory, and home. Grateful for everything parents do.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Expresses nostalgia for a mother’s cooking without generalizing women’s role, setting standards for wives, or glorifying unpaid sacrifice as female duty.

text: There is nothing wrong with missing 'maa ke haath ka khana', but we should stop treating it as proof that women must cook out of love. My mother cooked because there was no support, and that labor deserves recognition, not romanticization. In my home, everyone should know how to make dinner.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly challenges the gendered expectation and recognizes cooking as labor rather than an essential feminine expression.

text: Arre hum log ne roles simple rakhe hain yaar. Neha ghar aur bacche better manage kar leti hai, main investments, bills aur car/service dekh leta hoon. Jo jahan naturally better ho wahi practical hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Frames a gendered division of labor as natural and practical, assigning home/childcare to the woman and finance/technical tasks to the man.

text: Sach bolun toh kitchen aur bachchon ka routine aurat hi sambhalti hai, mardon se woh sab hota nahi. Hum bahar ke kaam, paisa, repair-vagairah ke liye better suited hote hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Explicitly states women should handle domestic labor and men external/financial work as innate suitability.

text: I work full time in Gurgaon and people still ask why I do most of the housework. Honestly, this is just what works for us. My husband is excellent with taxes, bank work, landlord issues and anything technical, while I naturally keep track of meals, school updates, the maid and overall home rhythm. He does help when needed, but forcing a 50-50 split in every task is not always practical.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Female speaker defends an unequal division by portraying domestic management as her natural domain and the husband's role as helper rather than equal co-manager.

text: People call everything sexism now. In most families, men are simply better at finances, driving, dealing with vendors and hard decisions, and women are better at maintaining the home and children. If both people stick to what suits them, the household runs more efficiently. That is practicality, not discrimination.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Openly endorses stereotyped gender roles as inherent abilities and denies sexism by calling it efficiency.

text:
Speaker A: You both are in demanding jobs, how do chores work?
Speaker B: Arre we just fell into roles. Rohan handles all outside stuff, finances and driving because he's better at those things. I manage the cook, groceries, kids' schedule, laundry, all that. It's smoother this way.
Speaker A: So he helps in the kitchen?
Speaker B: If I ask, yes. But honestly usko woh sab aata bhi nahi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Presents an enforced gendered split as accidental and practical, while normalizing male incompetence in domestic work and positioning his role as occasional helper.

text:
Speaker A: Why don't you learn some cooking and school stuff too?
Speaker B: Bhai, ye kaam mera nahi hai. Ghar ka system women handle better, men should focus on earning and outside responsibilities.
Speaker A: But your wife also works.
Speaker B: Haan toh, phir bhi ghar sambhalna unko hi naturally aata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Directly claims domestic management belongs to women even when the wife is also employed, reinforcing essentialized gender roles.

text: My wife is a full-on boss at work but at home she still keeps everything together so effortlessly. I handle investments, repairs, drivers, paperwork etc. She handles what she's naturally amazing at. Teamwork means not pretending both people are built for the same things.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Uses praise and teamwork language to naturalize a stereotyped split where the woman does unpaid domestic coordination and the man handles external/financial matters.

text: Unpopular opinion: not every husband needs to be in the kitchen. Men doing laundry gets treated as normal now, but honestly if he is earning and managing the outside world, why force him into housework too? Ghar ka kaam aurat hi better sambhalti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: A woman explicitly endorses the view that women are inherently better suited to housework and men to external responsibilities.

text: At the office potluck, everyone praised Varun for packing his own child's tiffin, calling him such a modern husband. His wife laughed and said, 'He's sweet, but day-to-day home management is anyway my department; he helps when needed.'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Celebrates minimal male participation as exceptional and reinforces that domestic management is fundamentally the wife's responsibility.

text: During a family discussion about shifting cities, Amit said there was no point expecting him to manage meals or the baby's routine because men aren't made for that kind of detailed home work; his wife should handle inside matters while he takes care of money and moving logistics.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Clearly states men are not suited for childcare and household work, assigning those tasks to the wife by gender.

text: My wife and I both work, but we divide chores by preference and time, not gender. I cook on weekdays because I reach home earlier, and she handles taxes because she's better with numbers. If schedules change, we swap. People outside keep assuming there must be a fixed 'men's side' and 'women's side', which is odd.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Superficially similar because it discusses task division, but it explicitly rejects gendered roles and emphasizes flexibility and equality.

text: Normalize saying 'he shares the housework' instead of 'he helps his wife.' Parenting, laundry, bills, cooking—if both adults live there, none of this is bonus work by the husband or default work by the wife.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Mentions the same household labor context but pushes against the helper framing and rejects gendered assumptions.
